Intestacy results in the court appointing an administrator (rather than the decedent choosing an executor), potentially higher costs, and distribution that may not match the decedent's wishes. Every estate plan should include, at minimum, a will, durable power of attorney, healthcare proxy, and living will.
Chapter 2: Wills, Trusts, and Probate

subsections.title: 2.1 Types of Trusts
paragraphs: A revocable living trust is the cornerstone of many estate plans. The grantor creates the trust, transfers assets into it, serves as trustee during life, and names successor trustees and beneficiaries. Because the grantor retains control, the trust assets are included in the taxable estate and the trust is a grantor trust for income tax purposes (all income is reported on the grantor's personal return). The primary benefits are probate avoidance, privacy, incapacity planning (the successor trustee manages assets if the grantor becomes incapacitated), and continuity of asset management.

Irrevocable trusts remove assets from the grantor's taxable estate because the grantor relinquishes all control. The transfer to an irrevocable trust is a completed gift, potentially subject to gift tax. Common irrevocable trusts include: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) — holds life insurance outside the estate (subject to the 3-year lookback rule for transferred policies); Credit Shelter Trust (CST) / Bypass Trust — preserves the deceased spouse's exemption amount while providing income to the surviving spouse; Qualified Personal Residence Trust (QPRT) — transfers a home at a discounted gift tax value; and Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT) — transfers appreciating assets at reduced gift tax cost.

subsections.title: 2.2 Special Purpose Trusts
paragraphs: Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) provide income to the donor (or other non-charitable beneficiary) for life or a term of years, with the remainder going to charity. The donor receives an immediate income tax deduction for the present value of the charitable remainder interest. A Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust (CRAT) pays a fixed dollar amount annually. A Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) pays a fixed percentage of the trust's annually revalued assets. CRTs are tax-exempt entities — they pay no income tax on gains from selling appreciated assets, making them excellent vehicles for diversifying concentrated stock positions.

Charitable Lead Trusts (CLTs) are the reverse of CRTs: they provide income to charity for a specified period, with the remainder going to family members. The gift/estate tax value of the remainder interest is reduced by the present value of the charitable income stream. Special Needs Trusts (SNTs) provide supplemental support for disabled beneficiaries without disqualifying them from government benefits (Medicaid, SSI). Spendthrift trusts include provisions preventing beneficiaries from assigning their interests and protecting trust assets from beneficiaries' creditors.
Chapter 3: Gift and Estate Taxation

subsections.title: 3.1 Gift Tax
paragraphs: The federal gift tax applies to transfers of property during life for less than full consideration. The annual gift tax exclusion for 2026 is $19,000 per donee ($38,000 for married couples using gift-splitting). Gifts within the annual exclusion do not reduce the lifetime exemption and do not require a gift tax return (unless gift-splitting is elected, which requires Form 709). The lifetime gift and estate tax exemption is $15 million per person (2026, OBBBA). Gifts exceeding the annual exclusion reduce the lifetime exemption dollar-for-dollar. The unlimited marital deduction allows unlimited gifts between U.S. citizen spouses. For non-citizen spouses, the annual exclusion is $185,000 (2026). Gifts to qualified charities are fully deductible with no limit.

Present interest gifts qualify for the annual exclusion. Future interest gifts (where the donee cannot immediately use or enjoy the property) do not qualify. Gifts to irrevocable trusts are generally future interest gifts unless the trust includes Crummey withdrawal powers, which give beneficiaries a temporary right to withdraw contributions, converting them to present interest gifts eligible for the annual exclusion. The 5-year gift tax averaging provision for 529 plans allows a lump-sum contribution of up to $95,000 ($190,000 for couples) to be treated as if made over 5 years for gift tax purposes.

subsections.title: 3.2 Estate Tax
paragraphs: The federal estate tax applies to the transfer of property at death. The gross estate includes all assets owned by the decedent at death (at fair market value), plus life insurance proceeds on policies owned by the decedent, the decedent's share of jointly held property, property over which the decedent held a general power of appointment, and certain transfers made within 3 years of death (including life insurance policies transferred to an ILIT). The taxable estate equals the gross estate minus deductions for debts, funeral expenses, administration costs, the marital deduction (unlimited for U.S. citizen spouses), and the charitable deduction (unlimited).

The estate tax exemption is $15 million per person (2026). Portability allows the surviving spouse to use the deceased spouse's unused exemption amount (DSUE), effectively doubling the exemption to $30 million for married couples. To elect portability, the executor must file a timely estate tax return (Form 706), even if no tax is due. The step-up in basis at death resets the cost basis of inherited assets to fair market value, permanently eliminating unrealized capital gains. This is one of the most significant tax benefits in the entire tax code.
Chapter 4: Advanced Transfer Techniques

Grantor Retained Annuity Trusts (GRATs) allow the grantor to transfer appreciating assets to beneficiaries at a reduced gift tax cost. The grantor contributes assets to the trust and retains an annuity payment for a specified term. The gift tax value is the value of the contributed assets minus the present value of the retained annuity (calculated using the Section 7520 rate). If the assets appreciate faster than the 7520 rate, the excess passes to beneficiaries gift-tax-free. A zeroed-out GRAT sets the annuity payments equal to the full value of the contributed assets, resulting in a gift tax value of approximately zero.

Family Limited Partnerships (FLPs) and Family LLCs allow parents to transfer business or investment assets to children at discounted values. The parents contribute assets to the entity and gift limited partnership interests to children. Because limited partners lack control and marketability, the interests are valued at a discount (typically 20-40% combined discount for lack of control and lack of marketability). The IRS scrutinizes FLPs closely, and the entity must have a legitimate business purpose beyond tax reduction. Installment sales to Intentionally Defective Grantor Trusts (IDGTs) allow the grantor to sell appreciating assets to the trust in exchange for a promissory note, freezing the value for estate tax purposes while the grantor continues to pay income tax on the trust's income (further reducing the estate).
Chapter 5: Business Succession Planning

Business succession planning ensures the orderly transfer of a business at the owner's retirement, disability, or death. Buy-sell agreements are the cornerstone of business succession planning. A cross-purchase agreement has the remaining owners buy the departing owner's interest (each owner owns a policy on the other owners). An entity purchase (stock redemption) agreement has the business itself buy the departing owner's interest. Cross-purchase agreements provide a stepped-up basis to the purchasing owners; entity purchases do not. For businesses with many owners, an entity purchase is simpler (the business owns one policy per owner rather than each owner owning policies on every other owner).

Key person insurance protects the business against the financial loss resulting from the death or disability of a key employee. The business owns the policy, pays the premiums (not deductible), and receives the death benefit (tax-free). Section 303 stock redemptions allow a corporation to redeem stock from a deceased shareholder's estate to pay estate taxes and administration expenses, with the redemption treated as a sale (capital gain) rather than a dividend (ordinary income). Section 6166 allows estates consisting largely of closely held business interests to pay estate taxes in installments over up to 14 years.

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) Mechanics
chapters.intro:
The ILIT is one of the most powerful estate planning tools for high-net-worth individuals. The trust is established as an irrevocable trust that owns a life insurance policy on the grantor's life. Because the trust owns the policy (not the insured), the death benefit is excluded from the insured's taxable estate. The trustee applies for and owns the policy, pays premiums using gifts from the grantor, and distributes the death benefit to beneficiaries according to the trust terms.
Crummey withdrawal powers are essential for ILIT funding. When the grantor makes a gift to the ILIT to pay premiums, each beneficiary receives a written notice of their right to withdraw their share of the gift (typically for 30-60 days). This converts the gift from a future interest (which does not qualify for the annual exclusion) to a present interest (which does qualify). The annual exclusion ($19,000 per beneficiary in 2026) allows the grantor to fund the ILIT without using any lifetime exemption, provided there are enough beneficiaries.
The 3-year lookback rule (IRC Section 2035) provides that if the insured transfers an existing life insurance policy to an ILIT and dies within 3 years of the transfer, the death benefit is included in the insured's estate. To avoid this risk, the best practice is to have the ILIT trustee apply for and purchase a new policy rather than transferring an existing policy. If an existing policy must be transferred, the grantor must survive 3 years for the estate exclusion to be effective.
FORMULA   ILIT Estate Tax Savings Client has a $20 million estate and a $5 million life insurance policy owned personally. Without ILIT: estate = $25 million, exemption = $15 million, taxable = $10 million, tax = $4 million (40%). With ILIT: estate = $20 million, exemption = $15 million, taxable = $5 million, tax = $2 million. ILIT savings: $2 million in estate tax. The $5 million death benefit passes to beneficiaries entirely tax-free (both income tax and estate tax).

subsections.title: A.2 Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
paragraphs: A GRAT is an irrevocable trust in which the grantor transfers assets and retains the right to receive an annuity payment for a specified term of years. At the end of the term, the remaining trust assets pass to the beneficiaries (or remain in trust for their benefit). The gift tax value of the transfer is the value of the assets contributed minus the present value of the retained annuity interest (calculated using the Section 7520 rate, which is 120% of the mid-term AFR). If the assets appreciate faster than the 7520 rate, the excess appreciation passes to beneficiaries free of gift and estate tax.

A zeroed-out GRAT sets the annuity payments equal to the full value of the contributed assets (plus the 7520 rate of return), resulting in a gift tax value of approximately zero. This means the grantor can transfer potentially unlimited appreciation to beneficiaries without using any lifetime exemption. The risk: if the grantor dies during the GRAT term, the trust assets are included in the grantor's estate (the strategy fails, but the estate is no worse off). GRATs work best with assets expected to appreciate significantly (concentrated stock positions, pre-IPO shares) and when the 7520 rate is low.

SCENARIO   GRAT Example Client transfers $10 million of company stock to a 2-year zeroed-out GRAT when the 7520 rate is 5%. Annual annuity payment: approximately $5.378 million. If the stock appreciates 30% per year: Year 1 value $13M, pay $5.378M, remaining $7.622M. Year 2 value $9.909M, pay $5.378M, remaining $4.531M passes to beneficiaries gift-tax-free. The client transferred $4.531 million without using any exemption. If the stock only grows at 5%, the remainder is approximately zero (the GRAT 'zeroes out').

subsections.title: A.3 Charitable Planning Strategies
paragraphs: Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) come in two forms: the Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust (CRAT) pays a fixed annuity (at least 5% of the initial fair market value) to the income beneficiary. No additional contributions are permitted. The Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) pays a fixed percentage (at least 5%) of the trust's annually revalued assets. Additional contributions are permitted. Both provide an immediate income tax deduction for the present value of the charitable remainder interest (which must be at least 10% of the initial contribution).

CRTs are particularly effective for diversifying concentrated stock positions. The client contributes highly appreciated stock to the CRT. The CRT sells the stock without paying capital gains tax (CRTs are tax-exempt entities). The proceeds are reinvested in a diversified portfolio. The client receives income for life (or a term of years), and the charity receives the remainder. Without the CRT, selling the stock directly would trigger significant capital gains tax, reducing the amount available for reinvestment.

Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) provide a simpler alternative to private foundations for charitable giving. The donor makes an irrevocable contribution to the DAF (receiving an immediate tax deduction), then recommends grants to charities over time. DAFs have no minimum distribution requirement (unlike private foundations, which must distribute 5% annually), no excise tax on investment income, and minimal administrative burden. The donor retains advisory privileges but not legal control over the funds.

chapters.title: Appendix: Gift Tax Rules and Strategies
chapters.intro:
The federal gift tax applies to transfers of property during life for less than full and adequate consideration. The annual exclusion allows each donor to give up to $19,000 per recipient per year (2026) without gift tax or using any lifetime exemption. Married couples can split gifts, effectively doubling the exclusion to $38,000 per recipient. Gifts to a spouse who is a U.S. citizen qualify for the unlimited marital deduction. Gifts to a non-citizen spouse are limited to $185,000 annually (2026).
The lifetime gift and estate tax exemption is unified at $15 million per individual in 2026 (under the OBBBA extension). Gifts exceeding the annual exclusion reduce the lifetime exemption dollar-for-dollar. The gift tax rate is 40% on amounts exceeding the exemption. Certain transfers are not considered taxable gifts: payments made directly to educational institutions for tuition (not room and board), payments made directly to medical providers for medical expenses, transfers to political organizations, and transfers to qualifying charities.
Advanced gifting strategies include: annual exclusion gifting programs (systematic gifts to multiple family members to transfer wealth over time), Crummey trusts (irrevocable trusts with withdrawal powers that convert future interest gifts into present interest gifts qualifying for the annual exclusion), 529 plan superfunding (contributing 5 years of annual exclusions in a single year — $95,000 per beneficiary in 2026), and grantor trust strategies (the grantor pays income tax on trust income, effectively making additional tax-free gifts to the trust beneficiaries).

subsections.title: Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT)
paragraphs: The GSTT is a separate tax imposed on transfers that skip a generation — transfers to grandchildren, great-grandchildren, or unrelated individuals more than 37.5 years younger than the transferor. The GSTT rate is a flat 40% (the highest estate tax rate), applied in addition to any gift or estate tax. The GSTT exemption is $15 million per individual in 2026 (same as the estate tax exemption). Proper allocation of the GSTT exemption is critical to avoid the devastating impact of double taxation (estate tax plus GSTT).

There are three types of generation-skipping transfers: direct skips (transfers directly to a skip person, such as a gift to a grandchild), taxable distributions (distributions from a trust to a skip person), and taxable terminations (when the interest of a non-skip person in a trust terminates and only skip persons remain as beneficiaries). The GSTT exemption can be allocated to trusts to create 'GST-exempt' trusts that can benefit multiple generations without further transfer tax. Dynasty trusts leverage this by lasting for multiple generations in states that have abolished the rule against perpetuities.

subsections.title: Powers of Attorney and Advance Directives
paragraphs: A durable power of attorney (DPOA) authorizes an agent to act on behalf of the principal for financial matters. 'Durable' means the authority continues even if the principal becomes incapacitated (a non-durable POA terminates upon incapacity). A springing DPOA becomes effective only upon the principal's incapacity (as certified by one or more physicians). A general DPOA grants broad authority over all financial matters; a limited (special) DPOA grants authority only for specific transactions or time periods.

A healthcare power of attorney (healthcare proxy) authorizes an agent to make medical decisions when the principal cannot. A living will (advance directive) expresses the principal's wishes regarding life-sustaining treatment, artificial nutrition and hydration, and organ donation. A HIPAA authorization allows designated individuals to access the principal's protected health information. These documents should be reviewed and updated regularly, especially after life events such as marriage, divorce, or relocation to a different state.
Deep Dive: Business Succession and Charitable Planning

subsections.title: Business Succession Planning
paragraphs: Business succession planning ensures the orderly transfer of business ownership upon the owner's death, disability, or retirement. Buy-sell agreements are the cornerstone of business succession: they establish a predetermined price and terms for the transfer of business interests. Cross-purchase agreements allow surviving owners to purchase the deceased owner's interest (funded by life insurance policies each owner holds on the others). Entity-purchase (stock redemption) agreements allow the business entity itself to purchase the deceased owner's interest (funded by entity-owned life insurance).

For partnerships with N partners, a cross-purchase requires N x (N-1) policies, while an entity-purchase requires only N policies. For example, a 4-partner firm needs 12 policies under cross-purchase but only 4 under entity-purchase. However, cross-purchase provides a stepped-up basis to the surviving partners (the purchase price becomes their new basis), while entity-purchase does not increase the surviving partners' basis in their partnership interests. A Section 754 election can mitigate this disadvantage for entity-purchase agreements.

Valuation methods for buy-sell agreements include: book value (simple but may not reflect true value), capitalization of earnings (applies a capitalization rate to normalized earnings), discounted cash flow (projects future cash flows and discounts to present value), and fair market value determined by independent appraisal. The agreed-upon valuation in a buy-sell agreement can establish the estate tax value of the business interest if the agreement meets certain requirements: it must be a bona fide business arrangement, not a device to transfer property to family members for less than adequate consideration, and the terms must be comparable to similar arm's-length arrangements.
Deep Dive: Charitable Planning Strategies

subsections.title: Charitable Remainder Trusts and Charitable Lead Trusts
paragraphs: A Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) provides income to the donor (or other non-charitable beneficiaries) for life or a term of years, with the remainder passing to charity. The donor receives an immediate income tax deduction for the present value of the charitable remainder interest. CRTs are exempt from income tax, so assets can be sold within the trust without triggering capital gains tax — making them ideal for highly appreciated assets. A Charitable Remainder Annuity Trust (CRAT) pays a fixed annuity amount (at least 5% of initial trust value). A Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) pays a fixed percentage (at least 5%) of the trust value as revalued annually.

A Charitable Lead Trust (CLT) is the reverse: charity receives income for a specified period, with the remainder passing to non-charitable beneficiaries (typically family members). CLTs are effective for transferring wealth to the next generation at a reduced gift/estate tax cost. A Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT) pays a fixed annuity to charity — if the trust assets grow faster than the IRS Section 7520 rate used to calculate the gift tax value, the excess passes to the remainder beneficiaries gift-tax-free. Donor-advised funds (DAFs) offer a simpler alternative: the donor makes an irrevocable contribution, receives an immediate deduction, and recommends grants to charities over time.

subsections.title: Estate Planning Exam Review: Key Numbers
paragraphs: Critical 2026 estate planning numbers: Federal estate/gift tax exemption: $15 million per individual ($30 million married with portability). Annual gift tax exclusion: $19,000 per recipient ($38,000 with gift splitting). GSTT exemption: $15 million per individual. Maximum estate/gift/GSTT tax rate: 40%. Gift to non-citizen spouse annual exclusion: $185,000. Charitable deduction: unlimited for estate tax purposes. Marital deduction: unlimited for U.S. citizen spouses. These numbers are indexed for inflation and may change annually.

KEY INSIGHT   Portability The surviving spouse can use the deceased spouse's unused estate tax exemption (DSUE amount) by filing a timely estate tax return (Form 706) even if no tax is due. This effectively doubles the exemption for married couples to $30 million without the need for credit shelter trusts. However, portability does NOT apply to the GSTT exemption — dynasty trust planning is still necessary to preserve both spouses' GSTT exemptions.

subsections.title: Estate Planning Final Review
paragraphs: The stepped-up basis rule (IRC Section 1014) adjusts the cost basis of inherited property to its fair market value at the date of death. This eliminates all unrealized capital gains that accrued during the decedent's lifetime. For example, if the decedent purchased stock for $10,000 and it was worth $100,000 at death, the heir's basis is $100,000 — if sold immediately, there is no capital gain. This is one of the most significant tax benefits in the tax code and is a key consideration in estate planning: highly appreciated assets should generally be held until death rather than gifted during life (gifts carry over the donor's basis).

Estate planning is an ongoing process, not a one-time event. Plans should be reviewed at least every 3-5 years and whenever a significant life event occurs: marriage, divorce, birth of a child or grandchild, death of a beneficiary, significant change in assets, change in state of residence, or changes in tax law. The 2026 estate tax exemption of $15 million is scheduled to sunset after 2025 under current law projections, potentially reverting to approximately $7 million (adjusted for inflation). Clients with estates between $7 million and $15 million should plan now to take advantage of the current higher exemption through lifetime gifting strategies.
Figure 13: Trust Selection Decision Tree
Figure 14: GRAT Mechanics Flow

practice_questions.prompt: The 2026 federal estate tax exemption per person is:
practice_questions.explanation: The OBBBA permanently set the exemption at $15,000,000 per person, indexed for inflation beginning 2027.

practice_questions.prompt: Which trust removes assets from the taxable estate?
options: Revocable living trust
Irrevocable trust
Grantor trust
Testamentary trust
practice_questions.explanation: Irrevocable trusts remove assets from the estate because the grantor gives up control. Revocable trusts remain in the estate.

practice_questions.prompt: The annual gift tax exclusion for 2026 is:
practice_questions.explanation: The 2026 annual gift tax exclusion is $19,000 per donee ($38,000 for married couples using gift-splitting).

practice_questions.prompt: Portability allows:
options: Transfer of assets between spouses tax-free
Use of the deceased spouse's unused exemption
Avoidance of probate
Transfer of property to minors
practice_questions.explanation: Portability allows the surviving spouse to use the DSUE (Deceased Spouse's Unused Exclusion). Requires filing Form 706.

practice_questions.prompt: The 3-year lookback rule applies to:
options: All gifts made within 3 years of death
Transfers of life insurance policies to ILITs
Charitable contributions
Annual exclusion gifts
practice_questions.explanation: The 3-year lookback specifically applies to transfers of life insurance policies. If the insured dies within 3 years of transferring a policy to an ILIT, the death benefit is included in the estate.

practice_questions.prompt: A zeroed-out GRAT results in a gift tax value of approximately:
options: The full value of contributed assets
50% of contributed assets
Zero
The Section 7520 rate times the contributed assets
practice_questions.explanation: A zeroed-out GRAT sets annuity payments equal to the full value plus the 7520 return, resulting in approximately zero gift tax value. Any appreciation above the 7520 rate passes tax-free.

practice_questions.prompt: The minimum payout rate for a Charitable Remainder Trust is:
practice_questions.explanation: CRTs must pay at least 5% of the initial value (CRAT) or annually revalued assets (CRUT) to the income beneficiary. The charitable remainder must be at least 10% of the initial contribution.

flashcards.definition: Online accounts and files, including email, social media, cloud storage, cryptocurrencies, and NFTs. Planning is crucial to avoid loss of access.
Revised Uniform Fiduciary Access to Digital Assets Act. Provides a legal framework for fiduciaries to access and manage digital assets.
A tax levied on the total value of a decedent's estate before distribution to beneficiaries. 2026 federal exemption: $15 million per person (OBBBA).
A tax paid by the beneficiaries who receive property from an estate. The rate often depends on the beneficiary's relationship to the decedent. Distinct from estate tax, which is paid by the estate.

Setting Every Community Up for Retirement Enhancement Act. Fundamentally altered the rules for beneficiaries of inherited retirement accounts, replacing the stretch IRA with a 10-year rule for most non-spouse beneficiaries.
Requires the entire balance of an inherited retirement account to be distributed by the end of the 10th year following the original owner's death for most non-spouse beneficiaries.

Beneficiaries exempt from the 10-year rule who can still use the life expectancy payout. Includes surviving spouses, minor children of the decedent, disabled individuals, chronically ill individuals, and beneficiaries not more than 10 years younger than the decedent.
A long-term trust created to pass wealth from generation to generation without incurring transfer taxes, such as estate tax or generation-skipping transfer (GST) tax, at each generational level.
A type of trust designed to protect a person's assets from claims of future creditors. Domestic APTs are available in some states but less protective than offshore trusts.
A Special Needs Trust funded with the assets of the disabled individual. It requires a payback provision to reimburse the state for Medicaid benefits upon the beneficiary's death.
A Special Needs Trust funded with the assets of someone other than the disabled individual, such as a parent or grandparent. It does not have a payback provision.

An irrevocable trust where the grantor transfers appreciating assets but retains the right to receive a fixed annuity for a term of years. Remaining assets pass to beneficiaries tax-free if appreciation exceeds the Section 7520 rate.

An irrevocable trust treated as a grantor trust for income tax but excluded from the estate. The grantor sells assets to the trust for a note at the AFR, freezing the value in the estate while appreciation passes to beneficiaries.
A trust created during the grantor's lifetime that can be amended or revoked. Avoids probate but does NOT provide estate tax reduction because assets remain in the grantor's taxable estate.
A trust that generally cannot be modified or revoked after creation. Assets are removed from the grantor's taxable estate but the grantor loses control.
The court-supervised process of validating a will, paying debts, and distributing assets. Assets held in trusts, jointly titled property, and accounts with beneficiary designations bypass probate.
Dying without a valid will. State law determines how assets are distributed. Varies by state and may not align with the decedent's wishes.
$19,000 per donee per year. Married couples can gift-split to give $38,000 per donee. Gifts within the exclusion do not reduce the lifetime exemption.
$15 million per person ($30 million per married couple with portability). Made permanent by the OBBBA. Covers both estate tax and lifetime gift tax.
The ability of a surviving spouse to use the deceased spouse's unused estate tax exemption (DSUE). Must file a timely estate tax return (Form 706) to elect portability, even if no tax is owed.

A tax on transfers to beneficiaries two or more generations below the transferor, designed to prevent wealthy families from avoiding estate tax by skipping a generation. 2026 exemption: $15 million (same as estate tax).
Unlimited deduction for transfers between spouses who are U.S. citizens. Transfers to non-citizen spouses are limited ($194,000 annual exclusion in 2026) and may require a QDOT.
An irrevocable trust that provides income to the grantor or named beneficiaries for a term, with the remainder going to a qualified charity. Provides a partial income tax deduction at creation.
An irrevocable trust that pays income to a charity for a term, with the remainder going to non-charitable beneficiaries (often family). Reduces gift/estate tax on the remainder interest.

A trust that owns a life insurance policy, removing the death benefit from the insured's taxable estate. Must observe the 3-year lookback rule for transferred policies and Crummey notice requirements.

The right of a trust beneficiary to withdraw contributions to the trust for a limited period (typically 30-60 days). Converts gifts to the trust into present-interest gifts qualifying for the annual exclusion.
At death, inherited assets receive a new cost basis equal to fair market value on the date of death. Eliminates unrealized capital gains. Preserved under OBBBA.
A legal document granting authority to act on someone's behalf. Durable POA survives incapacity. Springing POA activates upon incapacity.
Documents that designate someone to make healthcare decisions if the principal becomes incapacitated. Includes living wills specifying treatment preferences.
Required for estates passing to non-citizen surviving spouses to qualify for the marital deduction. Assets are subject to estate tax when distributed to the non-citizen spouse.

A GRAT structured so the present value of the annuity payments equals the value of the assets transferred, resulting in a taxable gift of zero. If assets outperform the 7520 rate, excess passes to beneficiaries tax-free.
The IRS-published interest rate used to value annuities, life interests, and remainders for tax purposes. Based on 120% of the midterm AFR. Critical for GRAT, CRT, and CLT calculations.

tracks.name: Premium Financing
tracks.title: Premium Financing
tracks.subtitle: Premium Financing Study Manual Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Premium Financing Mechanics
chapters.intro:
Premium financing is a lending arrangement where a third-party lender provides funds to pay insurance premiums. The insured makes a down payment (typically 20-30%) and the lender pays the balance directly to the insurer. The insured repays in monthly installments plus interest. The lender takes a security interest in the policy (unearned premium and cash value) as collateral. In Arizona, premium finance companies must be licensed by DIFI.
The agreement must disclose: total premium, down payment, amount financed, finance charge, APR, installment amounts, and cancellation provisions. On default, the lender can cancel the policy (with 10 days' notice to the insured) and receive the unearned premium refund. If the refund exceeds the loan balance, excess is returned to the insured; if less, the lender can pursue the deficiency.

subsections.title: A.2 Life Insurance Premium Financing for HNW Clients
paragraphs: Life insurance premium financing is a sophisticated HNW strategy: borrow funds to pay premiums on large permanent policies ($5M-$100M+ death benefit). The policy's cash value and death benefit serve as collateral, often supplemented by additional assets. Economics depend on the spread between policy returns and borrowing costs. When rates are low, the spread is favorable; when rates rise, it may require additional collateral or out-of-pocket payments.

Exit strategies are critical: (1) cash value grows to repay the loan (10-15 years), (2) periodic payments reduce the balance, (3) other assets repay at a predetermined point, or (4) death benefit repays at death. The worst case (rising rates, poor performance, declining collateral) can trigger collateral calls. STOLI arrangements (Stranger-Originated Life Insurance) are prohibited because they violate insurable interest requirements. Arizona requires a 2-year waiting period before a policy can be sold in a life settlement.

subsections.title: 11. The lender's primary collateral in premium financing is:
paragraphs: A) The insured's homeB) The insurance policyC) Investment accounts onlyD) A personal guarantee only
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: B The lender takes a security interest in the policy (unearned premium and cash value).

subsections.title: 12. The primary risk of life insurance premium financing is:
paragraphs: A) Insured dying too soonB) Interest rate riskC) Insurer insolvencyD) Tax law changes
MASTERY CHECK   Answer: B Rising rates increase borrowing costs while policy returns may not keep pace.
Deep Dive: Premium Financing Compliance and Case Studies

chapters.title: Appendix: Arizona Premium Finance Regulation
chapters.intro:
Arizona regulates premium financing under ARS Title 20, Chapter 6, Article 14. Premium finance companies must obtain a license from DIFI before conducting business. License requirements include: filing an application with DIFI, maintaining minimum net worth requirements, posting a surety bond, and designating a registered agent in Arizona. The license must be renewed annually. DIFI has the authority to examine the books and records of licensed premium finance companies and to take enforcement action for violations.
The premium finance agreement must contain specific disclosures required by Arizona law: the total premium amount, the amount of the down payment, the principal balance (amount financed), the finance charge in dollars, the annual percentage rate (APR), the number, amount, and due dates of installments, a description of the insurance policies being financed, the cancellation provisions and the insured's rights upon cancellation, and a statement that the premium finance company will have a security interest in the policy. The agreement must be signed by the insured (or their authorized representative) before the premium finance company advances funds to the insurer.
11. In Arizona, a premium finance company must be licensed by: A) The SEC B) FINRA C) The Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions (DIFI) D) The Federal Reserve. Answer: C. Arizona premium finance companies must be licensed by DIFI under ARS Title 20, Chapter 6, Article 14. They must maintain minimum net worth, post a surety bond, and comply with disclosure requirements.

subsections.title: Case Study: HNW Life Insurance Premium Financing
paragraphs: Consider a 55-year-old business owner with a $30 million estate who needs $10 million of permanent life insurance for estate liquidity. Annual premium: $200,000 for a guaranteed universal life policy. Without premium financing, the client pays $200,000/year from cash flow or investments. With premium financing: the client makes a 25% down payment ($50,000) and finances the remaining $150,000. At a 5% interest rate, the annual interest cost is approximately $7,500. The client's investment portfolio earns 8% annually, so the $150,000 kept invested earns approximately $12,000 — a positive arbitrage of $4,500.

The key risks in this scenario: if interest rates rise to 8%, the arbitrage disappears and the client is paying more in interest than they would earn by investing the $150,000. If the client's investment portfolio declines, the lender may require additional collateral. If the policy underperforms (lower cash value than projected), the exit strategy may be delayed. The adviser must stress-test the arrangement under adverse scenarios (rising rates, poor investment returns, declining policy performance) and ensure the client understands and can absorb the downside risks.
Deep Dive: Risk Mitigation and Exit Strategies

subsections.title: Risk Mitigation in Premium Financing
paragraphs: Effective risk mitigation in premium financing requires addressing multiple risk factors. Interest rate risk can be mitigated through: fixed-rate loans (locking in the borrowing cost), interest rate caps (limiting the maximum rate), and shorter loan terms (reducing exposure to rate fluctuations). Collateral risk is managed through: conservative loan-to-value ratios (typically 70-80% of policy cash value), diversified collateral (not relying solely on the policy), and regular collateral monitoring with predetermined trigger points for additional collateral calls.

Policy performance risk (the risk that the policy's cash value does not grow as projected) is mitigated through: conservative illustration assumptions (using current rates rather than maximum non-guaranteed rates), guaranteed universal life policies (which provide guaranteed death benefits regardless of market performance), and regular policy reviews with the ability to adjust the financing structure. The adviser should present stress-test scenarios showing the impact of: interest rates 200-300 basis points above current levels, policy returns at the guaranteed minimum, and simultaneous adverse movements in both variables.
Deep Dive: Regulatory Compliance and Consumer Protection

subsections.title: Consumer Protection in Premium Financing
paragraphs: Premium financing regulations are designed to protect consumers from predatory lending practices. Key consumer protections include: the right to cancel the premium finance agreement within a specified period (typically 10 days) without penalty, the right to receive clear and conspicuous disclosure of all terms and costs before signing, the right to receive notice before the premium finance company cancels the underlying insurance policy (typically 10 days' notice to the insured and the insurer), and the right to receive any unearned premium refund that exceeds the outstanding loan balance. The premium finance company cannot charge a prepayment penalty if the insured pays off the loan early.

Arizona law requires premium finance companies to file their rates and forms with DIFI. The maximum service charge is regulated and varies based on the amount financed and the term of the agreement. Premium finance companies are subject to examination by DIFI, which reviews: compliance with disclosure requirements, accuracy of APR calculations, proper handling of cancellation and refund procedures, adequacy of record-keeping, and treatment of consumer complaints. Violations can result in administrative penalties, license revocation, and civil liability to affected consumers.

subsections.title: Premium Financing Summary and Key Takeaways
paragraphs: Premium financing is a specialized financial tool that serves both commercial insurance clients (who need to preserve cash flow by financing large commercial premiums) and high-net-worth individuals (who use life insurance premium financing as a wealth transfer strategy). The key to successful premium financing is understanding the economics: the cost of borrowing must be justified by either the cash flow benefit (commercial) or the arbitrage opportunity (HNW life insurance). Advisers must conduct thorough suitability analysis, stress-test the arrangement under adverse scenarios, and ensure the client fully understands the risks including interest rate risk, collateral risk, and policy performance risk.

practice_questions.prompt: The primary collateral for a premium-financed life insurance policy is:
options: The policyholder's home
The policy's cash value
The policyholder's investment portfolio
A bank guarantee
practice_questions.explanation: The policy's cash value serves as primary collateral, with additional assets (securities, letters of credit) as supplemental collateral.

practice_questions.prompt: STOLI arrangements are:
options: Encouraged for estate planning
Prohibited in most states
Required for large policies
Only available to accredited investors
practice_questions.explanation: Stranger-Originated Life Insurance (STOLI) arrangements, where an investor with no insurable interest initiates a policy, are prohibited in most states.

practice_questions.prompt: Commercial premium financing typically involves:
options: 3-4 monthly payments
9-10 monthly payments
24 monthly payments
A single balloon payment
practice_questions.explanation: Commercial premium financing typically spreads the annual premium over 9-10 monthly installments, preserving business cash flow.

practice_questions.prompt: The lender's primary collateral in premium financing is:
options: The insured's home
The insurance policy
Investment accounts only
A personal guarantee only
practice_questions.explanation: The lender takes a security interest in the policy (unearned premium and cash value).

practice_questions.prompt: The primary risk of life insurance premium financing is:
options: Insured dying too soon
Interest rate risk
Insurer insolvency
Tax law changes
practice_questions.explanation: Rising rates increase borrowing costs while policy returns may not keep pace.

flashcards.definition: Borrowing money from a third-party lender to pay life insurance premiums. Policy cash value serves as primary collateral.
Policy loan: insurance company lends against its own cash value. Premium financing: independent third-party lender provides loan with policy assigned as collateral.
The policy is assigned to the lender as security. Lender has first claim on death benefit or cash value up to the loan balance.
Secured Overnight Financing Rate. Replaced LIBOR as the benchmark for variable-rate premium finance loans. Loan rate is typically SOFR plus a spread.
The ratio of the loan amount to the collateral value. Lenders typically require LTV below 85-90%. If LTV rises, additional collateral may be required.
Assets beyond the policy cash value pledged to maintain required LTV ratios. Can include letters of credit, securities, or real estate.
Uniform Commercial Code filing that perfects the lender's security interest in the policy. Filed with the state to establish priority.
Plan for repaying the premium finance loan. Options include: policy cash value surrender, death benefit proceeds, refinancing, or repayment from other assets.
Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust often owns the policy in premium financing to keep death benefit outside the insured's taxable estate.
If the policy cash value drops below required LTV thresholds, the lender can demand additional collateral or loan repayment.
Rising interest rates increase the cost of the premium finance loan and can erode the arbitrage between policy returns and loan costs.
If the policy's credited interest rate or index returns underperform projections, cash value growth may be insufficient to support the exit strategy.
Not all banks offer premium financing. Specialty lenders evaluate the insured's net worth, the insurance carrier rating, and the policy structure.
Indexed Universal Life is commonly used because it offers upside potential with downside protection (floor), though caps limit maximum credited rate.
Split-dollar: employer/employee share policy costs and benefits. Premium financing: third-party lender provides loan. Different tax treatment and economic arrangements.
If an ILIT borrows for premiums, the trust's interest payments may be treated as gifts to the trust, requiring Crummey notices and using annual exclusions or lifetime exemption.
The employer owns the policy, and the economic benefit (term cost of insurance) is taxable income to the employee. Distinct from loan regime.
The employee owns the policy, and the employer's premium payments are treated as loans. Interest may need to be charged at the AFR.
IRS-published minimum interest rates for loans. Below-market loans may trigger imputed income or gift tax consequences.
Premium financing works when the policy's internal rate of return exceeds the loan interest rate, creating positive spread over time.
Lenders typically require the insurance carrier to have an A or A+ rating from AM Best to qualify for premium financing.
Typically 5-15 years. Shorter periods reduce interest costs but require higher collateral. Longer periods increase total borrowing costs.
If the insured dies during financing, the death benefit first repays the outstanding loan. Only the remainder passes to beneficiaries.
Generally appropriate for high-net-worth individuals with: liquid net worth of $5M+, need for large life insurance coverage, and desire to preserve liquidity.
Critical to stress-test policy illustrations at lower-than-projected crediting rates and higher-than-projected loan rates before committing to financing.
If the loan has annual renewal terms, the lender may choose not to renew, requiring the borrower to find alternative financing or repay the loan.
If the insured's net worth declines (e.g., market downturn), they may not have sufficient assets to meet collateral calls or repay the loan.
Businesses use premium financing to fund key person life insurance policies, preserving working capital while securing coverage.
With the 2026 OBBBA exemption at $15 million per person, premium financing for estate tax coverage is primarily for estates exceeding $30 million (married couples).
Life insurance policies must meet IRS Section 7702 (cash value accumulation test or guideline premium test) to retain tax-advantaged status.

tracks.name: Life & Health Insurance
tracks.title: Life & Health Insurance
tracks.subtitle: Life and Health Insurance Study Manual Comprehensive Study Guide Updated March 2026  |  OBBBA Integrated

chapters.title: Exam Overview
chapters.intro: Chapter 1: Life Insurance Fundamentals

subsections.title: 1.1 Principles of Insurance
paragraphs: Insurance is a risk transfer mechanism where the insured pays a premium to the insurer in exchange for a promise to pay benefits upon the occurrence of a covered loss. The law of large numbers allows insurers to predict losses with reasonable accuracy by pooling a large number of similar risks. Adverse selection is the tendency of higher-risk individuals to seek insurance more actively than lower-risk individuals. Insurers combat adverse selection through underwriting — the process of evaluating and classifying risk to determine whether to issue a policy and at what premium.

Insurable interest requires that the policyholder would suffer a financial loss from the insured event. For life insurance, insurable interest must exist at the time of application (not at the time of claim). A person always has insurable interest in their own life. Insurable interest also exists between spouses, parents and children, business partners, and employers and key employees. The principle of indemnity (applicable to property and health insurance, not life insurance) limits recovery to the actual financial loss suffered, preventing the insured from profiting from a loss.

subsections.title: 1.2 Needs Analysis and Underwriting
paragraphs: The human life value approach estimates the present value of the insured's future earnings, adjusted for taxes, personal consumption, and inflation. The needs approach (more commonly used) calculates specific financial obligations: final expenses ($10,000-$25,000), outstanding debts (mortgage, loans), income replacement (present value of needed income for dependents), education funding, emergency fund, and special needs. Existing resources (savings, investments, Social Security survivor benefits, existing insurance) are subtracted to determine the insurance gap.

Underwriting classifies applicants into risk categories: preferred (lowest risk, lowest premiums), standard (average risk, standard premiums), substandard/rated (higher risk, higher premiums), and declined (uninsurable). Factors considered include age, gender, health history, family medical history, occupation, hobbies, tobacco use, driving record, and financial situation. The Medical Information Bureau (MIB) maintains a database of medical conditions reported by member insurance companies to detect fraud and adverse selection.
Chapter 2: Life Insurance Products

subsections.title: 2.1 Term Life Insurance
paragraphs: Term life insurance provides pure death protection for a specified period (10, 15, 20, or 30 years) with no cash value accumulation. It is the most affordable type of life insurance and is appropriate for temporary needs such as income replacement during working years, mortgage protection, and coverage during child-rearing years. Level term maintains a constant death benefit and premium throughout the term. Decreasing term maintains a constant premium but the death benefit decreases over time (used for mortgage protection). Annual renewable term (ART) renews annually with increasing premiums based on the insured's attained age.

Convertible term policies allow the policyholder to convert to a permanent policy without evidence of insurability, typically within a specified conversion period. This is valuable because the insured's health may deteriorate during the term, making it impossible to qualify for a new policy. The conversion is based on the insured's attained age (current age) at the time of conversion, resulting in higher premiums than if the permanent policy had been purchased originally.

subsections.title: 2.2 Whole Life Insurance
paragraphs: Whole life (ordinary life) insurance provides permanent coverage for the insured's entire lifetime with guaranteed level premiums, a guaranteed death benefit, and guaranteed cash value growth. The cash value grows at a guaranteed minimum rate and can be accessed through policy loans or partial surrenders. Policy loans accrue interest but do not need to be repaid (outstanding loans reduce the death benefit). Whole life premiums are significantly higher than term premiums because a portion of each premium funds the cash value. Whole life is appropriate for permanent needs: estate liquidity, final expenses, wealth transfer, and supplemental retirement income.

Limited-pay whole life (such as 20-pay life or paid-up at 65) compresses the premium payments into a shorter period while providing lifetime coverage. Single-premium whole life is paid with one lump sum and is always classified as a Modified Endowment Contract (MEC). Participating whole life policies pay dividends (which are considered a return of premium and are not taxable until they exceed the total premiums paid). Dividends can be taken in cash, used to reduce premiums, left to accumulate at interest, or used to purchase paid-up additions (additional insurance).

subsections.title: 2.3 Universal and Variable Life
paragraphs: Universal life (UL) offers flexible premiums and an adjustable death benefit. The policyholder can increase or decrease premiums (within limits) and adjust the death benefit. Cash value earns interest based on current rates (with a guaranteed minimum). UL has two death benefit options: Option A (level death benefit, where the cash value is included in the death benefit) and Option B (increasing death benefit, where the death benefit equals the face amount plus the cash value). Option B is more expensive but provides increasing coverage.

Variable life (VL) allows the policyholder to invest the cash value in subaccounts (similar to mutual funds), offering higher growth potential but with investment risk. The death benefit has a guaranteed minimum but can increase if investments perform well. Variable universal life (VUL) combines the flexibility of UL with the investment options of VL. Both VL and VUL are securities and require a securities license (Series 6 or Series 7) in addition to a life insurance license. Indexed universal life (IUL) credits interest based on a market index (such as the S&P 500) with a floor (minimum rate, often 0-2%) and a cap (maximum rate, often 8-12%).
Chapter 3: Life Insurance Policy Provisions

Standard policy provisions protect both the insurer and the policyholder. The free-look period (typically 10-30 days) allows the policyholder to return the policy for a full refund after delivery. The grace period (typically 31 days) allows premium payment after the due date without lapse. The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the policy after it has been in force for 2 years (except for non-payment of premiums). The suicide clause excludes death by suicide within the first 2 years of the policy (after 2 years, suicide is covered).

The misstatement of age provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit (rather than void the policy) if the insured's age was misstated on the application. The entire contract provision states that the policy and the application (attached to the policy) constitute the entire contract. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) with evidence of insurability and payment of back premiums with interest. Non-forfeiture options (for permanent policies) protect the policyholder's cash value if the policy lapses: cash surrender value, reduced paid-up insurance, or extended term insurance.

Settlement options determine how the death benefit is paid to the beneficiary. Lump sum provides the entire benefit at once. Fixed period pays equal installments over a specified number of years. Fixed amount pays a specified dollar amount until the proceeds are exhausted. Life income (annuitization) pays for the beneficiary's lifetime. Interest only leaves the proceeds with the insurer, paying only the interest to the beneficiary. The beneficiary designation can be revocable (the policyholder can change it) or irrevocable (requires the beneficiary's consent to change).
Chapter 4: Annuities

An annuity is a contract between an individual and an insurance company designed to provide income, typically during retirement. During the accumulation phase, the contract owner makes premium payments and the value grows tax-deferred. During the annuitization (payout) phase, the insurance company makes periodic payments to the annuitant. Fixed annuities guarantee a minimum interest rate and provide predictable payments. Variable annuities invest in subaccounts with returns dependent on market performance — they are securities requiring a securities license.

Annuity payout options include: life only (payments for the annuitant's lifetime, highest payment but nothing to beneficiaries at death), life with period certain (payments for life with a guaranteed minimum period, such as 10 or 20 years), joint and survivor (payments for two lives, continuing to the survivor at 50-100% of the original amount), and fixed period or fixed amount. The exclusion ratio determines the tax treatment of each payment: Exclusion Ratio = Investment in Contract / Expected Return. The excluded portion is a tax-free return of basis; the remainder is taxable as ordinary income.

Taxation of annuities: contributions to non-qualified annuities are made with after-tax dollars (no deduction). Growth is tax-deferred. Withdrawals during accumulation are taxed LIFO (earnings first, as ordinary income) with a 10% penalty before age 59 1/2. Qualified annuities (held in IRAs or employer plans) follow the same distribution rules as the underlying retirement account. The 1035 exchange allows tax-free exchange of one annuity for another, or a life insurance policy for an annuity (but NOT an annuity for a life insurance policy).
Chapter 5: Health Insurance Fundamentals

Health insurance protects against the financial consequences of illness and injury. Major medical insurance covers a broad range of medical expenses with a deductible (the amount the insured pays before coverage begins), coinsurance (the percentage split between insurer and insured, typically 80/20), and an out-of-pocket maximum (the most the insured pays in a year, after which the insurer pays 100%). The Affordable Care Act (ACA) established essential health benefits that all individual and small group plans must cover, prohibited denial of coverage for pre-existing conditions, and eliminated lifetime benefit limits.

Disability income insurance replaces a portion of income (typically 60-70%) when the insured cannot work due to illness or injury. Own-occupation coverage pays if the insured cannot perform their specific occupation. Any-occupation coverage pays only if the insured cannot perform any occupation for which they are reasonably qualified. The elimination period (waiting period) is the time between disability onset and benefit commencement — longer elimination periods reduce premiums. Tax treatment: employer-paid premiums result in taxable benefits; employee-paid (after-tax) premiums result in tax-free benefits.
Chapter 6: Health Insurance Products

Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers custodial care, assisted living, home health care, and skilled nursing facility care. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 or more activities of daily living (ADLs: bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, continence) or has a cognitive impairment. Tax-qualified LTC policies allow premium deductions as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits) and provide tax-free benefits up to a per-diem limit ($420/day in 2026). Partnership policies protect assets equal to benefits received from Medicaid spend-down requirements.

Medicare is the federal health insurance program for individuals age 65+ and certain disabled individuals. Part A covers hospital care (funded by payroll taxes, no premium for most). Part B covers physician services and outpatient care (monthly premium, approximately $185/month in 2026). Part C (Medicare Advantage) is a private alternative to Parts A and B. Part D covers prescription drugs. Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policies fill the gaps in Original Medicare coverage. Medicare does NOT cover long-term custodial care, dental, vision, or hearing (with limited exceptions under Medicare Advantage).
Chapter 7: Insurance Regulation and Ethics

Insurance is regulated primarily at the state level. Each state has an insurance department headed by a commissioner (or superintendent) who oversees licensing, market conduct, rate approval, and consumer protection. The McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945) affirmed state regulation of insurance and exempted insurance from most federal antitrust laws. Producers (agents and brokers) must be licensed in each state where they sell insurance. Continuing education is required for license renewal (typically 24 hours per renewal period, including ethics).

Unfair trade practices include misrepresentation (making false statements about a policy), twisting (using misrepresentation to induce a policyholder to replace an existing policy), churning (replacing policies to generate commissions without benefit to the client), rebating (giving part of the commission to the client as an inducement to purchase), and defamation (making false statements about a competitor). Replacement regulations require the agent to provide a comparison of the existing and proposed policies and to notify the existing insurer of the replacement. The agent has a duty to ensure that the replacement is in the client's best interest.

rows: Pre-licensing education (varies by state, typically 20-40 hours)

chapters.title: Appendix: A.1 Life Insurance Policy Provisions
chapters.intro:
The free-look provision gives the policyowner a specified period (typically 10-30 days, depending on state law) after policy delivery to examine the policy and return it for a full refund of premiums paid. The grace period (typically 31 days for annual premium policies, 10 days for monthly) allows the policyowner to make a late premium payment without the policy lapsing. If the insured dies during the grace period, the death benefit is paid minus the overdue premium.
The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the validity of the policy after it has been in force for a specified period (typically 2 years from the date of issue). After the contestability period, the insurer cannot void the policy even if the application contained material misrepresentations — with the exception of fraud (in most states). The misstatement of age or sex provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit (not void the policy) if the insured's age or sex was misstated on the application.
The entire contract clause states that the policy and the attached application constitute the entire contract between the parties. No statement made by the applicant can be used to void the policy unless it is contained in the written application attached to the policy. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) if the policyowner pays all back premiums with interest, provides evidence of insurability, and pays any outstanding policy loans. A reinstated policy has a new contestability period and a new suicide exclusion period.

subsections.title: A.2 Life Insurance Riders
paragraphs: The waiver of premium rider waives premium payments if the insured becomes totally disabled (as defined in the rider) for a specified waiting period (typically 6 months). The policy remains in force as if premiums were being paid. The guaranteed insurability rider (guaranteed purchase option) allows the insured to purchase additional coverage at specified future dates (typically every 3 years or at life events such as marriage or birth of a child) without evidence of insurability. The accelerated death benefit rider (living benefit) allows the insured to receive a portion of the death benefit (typically 25-75%) while still living if diagnosed with a terminal illness (life expectancy of 12-24 months).

The accidental death benefit rider (double indemnity) pays an additional death benefit (typically equal to the face amount) if the insured dies as a result of an accident. The death must occur within a specified period after the accident (typically 90 days). Exclusions typically include death from war, aviation (as a pilot), and self-inflicted injuries. The term rider adds term insurance coverage to a permanent policy, providing additional temporary coverage at a lower cost than purchasing a separate term policy. The children's term rider provides term coverage for all children of the insured, typically converting to permanent coverage at age 25.

subsections.title: A.3 Health Insurance Fundamentals
paragraphs: Health insurance provides coverage for medical expenses, including hospitalization, surgery, physician visits, prescription drugs, and preventive care. The Affordable Care Act (ACA) established key requirements: guaranteed issue (insurers cannot deny coverage based on pre-existing conditions), community rating (premiums can vary only by age, tobacco use, family size, and geographic area), essential health benefits (10 categories of coverage that all ACA-compliant plans must include), and the metal tier system (Bronze: 60% actuarial value, Silver: 70%, Gold: 80%, Platinum: 90%).

Health Savings Accounts (HSAs) are available to individuals enrolled in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP). For 2026, the minimum deductible is $1,700 (individual) / $3,400 (family), and the maximum out-of-pocket is $8,500 (individual) / $17,000 (family). HSA contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free (triple tax benefit). After age 65, HSA withdrawals for non-medical expenses are taxed as ordinary income (no penalty). Flexible Spending Accounts (FSAs) allow employees to set aside pre-tax dollars for medical expenses, but FSAs have a use-it-or-lose-it provision (with a $640 carryover or 2.5-month grace period).

Medicare is the federal health insurance program for individuals age 65+ and certain disabled individuals. Part A (Hospital Insurance) covers inpatient hospital care, skilled nursing facility care, home health care, and hospice care. Part A is premium-free for individuals with 40+ quarters of Social Security coverage. Part B (Medical Insurance) covers physician services, outpatient care, preventive services, and durable medical equipment. Part B has a monthly premium ($185/month standard in 2026, higher for high-income individuals under IRMAA). Part D covers prescription drugs through private plans. Medicare Advantage (Part C) is an alternative to Original Medicare, offered by private insurers, combining Parts A, B, and usually D with additional benefits.

chapters.title: Appendix: Disability Insurance
chapters.intro:
Disability insurance replaces a portion of income (typically 60-70%) when the insured is unable to work due to illness or injury. Own-occupation coverage pays benefits if the insured cannot perform the duties of their specific occupation. Any-occupation coverage pays only if the insured cannot perform any occupation for which they are reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. Own-occupation is more expensive but provides broader protection, especially for professionals (physicians, attorneys) whose specialized skills command higher income.
Key disability policy features: the elimination period (waiting period before benefits begin, typically 30-180 days — longer elimination periods reduce premiums), the benefit period (how long benefits are paid, ranging from 2 years to age 65 or lifetime), residual/partial disability benefits (pays a proportional benefit when the insured can work part-time but earns less than before disability), cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) rider (increases benefits annually to keep pace with inflation), and the non-cancelable/guaranteed renewable distinction.
Non-cancelable policies guarantee that the insurer cannot cancel the policy, increase premiums, or change benefits as long as premiums are paid — this is the strongest form of disability coverage. Guaranteed renewable policies guarantee the insured can renew the policy, but the insurer can increase premiums for the entire class of policyholders (not individually). Conditionally renewable policies can be cancelled by the insurer under specified conditions. The tax treatment of disability benefits depends on who paid the premiums: employer-paid premiums result in taxable benefits; individually-paid premiums result in tax-free benefits.
Group life insurance is employer-sponsored coverage. Key features: no individual underwriting (guaranteed issue up to a specified amount), the employer is the policyholder and employees receive certificates of insurance, coverage is typically term insurance equal to 1-2x annual salary, and employees can convert to individual policies upon termination (conversion privilege). Under IRC Section 79, employer-paid group term life insurance premiums are tax-free to the employee for the first $50,000 of coverage. Premiums for coverage exceeding $50,000 are taxable income to the employee based on IRS Table I rates (imputed income).
Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers the cost of care for individuals who cannot perform activities of daily living (ADLs): bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, and continence. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 of 6 ADLs or has a severe cognitive impairment. The elimination period (typically 30-90 days) is the waiting period before benefits begin — longer elimination periods reduce premiums. LTC premiums may be tax-deductible as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits). Partnership policies allow the insured to protect assets equal to the benefits received from Medicaid spend-down requirements.
Continuing education is required to maintain a life and health insurance license. Most states require 24 hours of CE every 2 years, including ethics credits. Agents must also complete any state-specific requirements such as flood insurance CE or long-term care partnership program training. Failure to complete CE by the deadline results in license lapse, and the agent must cease all insurance activities until the license is reinstated.
Success on the life and health exam requires mastering both the technical insurance concepts and the state-specific regulatory requirements that govern agent conduct and consumer protection in your jurisdiction.
Figure 15: Life Insurance Product Spectrum
Figure 16: Insurance Needs Analysis Waterfall

subsections.title: Long-Term Care Insurance
paragraphs: Long-term care (LTC) insurance covers the cost of care when the insured needs assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs): bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring (moving in/out of bed/chair), and continence. Benefits are triggered when the insured cannot perform 2 of 6 ADLs or has a severe cognitive impairment (such as Alzheimer's disease). LTC policies typically have a daily or monthly benefit amount ($150-$400/day), a benefit period (2-5 years or lifetime), and an elimination period (30-180 days).

LTC coverage options include: nursing home care (the most expensive, averaging $8,000-$12,000/month), assisted living facility care ($4,000-$7,000/month), home health care (professional care provided in the insured's home), adult day care, and hospice care. Inflation protection riders are critical for LTC policies because the cost of care increases significantly over time — a 3-5% compound inflation rider ensures benefits keep pace with rising care costs. Partnership programs in many states allow policyholders to protect assets equal to the benefits received from a qualified LTC policy, providing Medicaid asset protection.

Tax-qualified LTC policies (meeting IRC Section 7702B requirements) provide tax benefits: premiums are deductible as medical expenses (subject to age-based limits and the 7.5% AGI threshold), and benefits received are tax-free up to the per diem limit ($420/day in 2026) or actual costs incurred. Non-tax-qualified policies may provide broader benefit triggers but do not receive favorable tax treatment. Hybrid LTC policies (combining life insurance or annuities with LTC benefits) have become increasingly popular as they guarantee a benefit even if LTC is never needed.

subsections.title: Group Insurance and Employee Benefits
paragraphs: Group life insurance is typically provided by employers as a benefit. Group term life is the most common form, providing coverage for the duration of employment. The employer can deduct premiums as a business expense. The first $50,000 of employer-provided group term life is tax-free to the employee; coverage above $50,000 results in imputed income based on IRS Table I rates. Group life is guaranteed issue (no individual underwriting) during the initial enrollment period, making it valuable for employees with health conditions who might not qualify for individual coverage.

COBRA (Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act) requires employers with 20+ employees to offer continuation of group health coverage to employees and dependents who lose coverage due to a qualifying event (termination, reduction in hours, divorce, death of the covered employee). COBRA coverage lasts 18 months for termination/reduction in hours, 36 months for divorce/death/Medicare eligibility. The former employee pays the full premium plus a 2% administrative fee. COBRA is a bridge to other coverage, not a long-term solution.
Deep Dive: Annuities and Health Insurance

subsections.title: Annuity Types and Taxation
paragraphs: Fixed annuities guarantee a minimum interest rate during the accumulation phase and a fixed payment during the annuitization phase. The insurer bears the investment risk. Immediate annuities begin payments within one year of purchase (single premium immediate annuity — SPIA). Deferred annuities accumulate value over time before payments begin. Annuity payout options include: life only (highest payment, no beneficiary), life with period certain (payments for life or a guaranteed period, whichever is longer), joint and survivor (payments continue for the surviving annuitant), and fixed period/fixed amount.

The exclusion ratio determines the tax-free portion of each annuity payment: Exclusion Ratio = Investment in Contract / Expected Return. For example, if an annuitant invested $100,000 and the expected return is $200,000, the exclusion ratio is 50% — half of each payment is tax-free (return of principal) and half is taxable (earnings). After the investment is fully recovered, all subsequent payments are fully taxable. For qualified annuities (funded with pre-tax dollars in retirement plans), the entire payment is taxable because no after-tax contributions were made.

The 1035 exchange allows tax-free exchanges between certain insurance products: life insurance to life insurance, life insurance to annuity, annuity to annuity, and life insurance to long-term care. An annuity CANNOT be exchanged tax-free for life insurance (this would convert tax-deferred gains into a tax-free death benefit). The exchange must be between the same owner and insured/annuitant. Partial 1035 exchanges are also permitted. This is a critical planning tool for clients who want to change products without triggering a taxable event.

subsections.title: Health Insurance Fundamentals
paragraphs: Health insurance plans are categorized by metal tier under the Affordable Care Act: Bronze (60% actuarial value — lowest premiums, highest out-of-pocket costs), Silver (70% AV), Gold (80% AV), and Platinum (90% AV — highest premiums, lowest out-of-pocket costs). High-Deductible Health Plans (HDHPs) qualify for Health Savings Account (HSA) contributions. 2026 HDHP minimums: $1,700 individual / $3,400 family deductible. 2026 HSA contribution limits: $4,400 individual / $8,750 family ($1,000 catch-up for age 55+).

HSAs offer triple tax advantages: contributions are tax-deductible (or pre-tax if through payroll), growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. Unlike FSAs, HSAs have no use-it-or-lose-it provision — funds roll over indefinitely. After age 65, HSA funds can be withdrawn for any purpose (taxed as ordinary income, like a traditional IRA) or tax-free for medical expenses. HSAs are individually owned and portable — they are not tied to an employer. This makes them a powerful retirement planning tool in addition to their healthcare function.
Deep Dive: Life Insurance Policy Provisions and Riders

subsections.title: Standard Policy Provisions
paragraphs: Life insurance policies contain standard provisions required by state law. The incontestability clause prevents the insurer from contesting the policy after it has been in force for 2 years (except for non-payment of premiums). Even material misrepresentations on the application cannot void the policy after the contestability period. The suicide clause excludes death benefits if the insured commits suicide within the first 2 years of the policy — after 2 years, suicide is covered. The misstatement of age provision allows the insurer to adjust the death benefit to what the premium would have purchased at the correct age, rather than voiding the policy.

The grace period (typically 31 days for annual premium policies) allows the policyholder to pay a late premium without losing coverage. If the insured dies during the grace period, the death benefit is paid minus the overdue premium. The reinstatement provision allows a lapsed policy to be reinstated within a specified period (typically 3-5 years) if the policyholder provides evidence of insurability and pays all back premiums with interest. A new contestability period begins upon reinstatement. The entire contract provision states that the policy and attached application constitute the entire contract — no external documents can modify the agreement.

Common life insurance riders include: waiver of premium (waives premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled — typically requires disability for 6 months with a retroactive waiver to the date of disability), accelerated death benefit (allows access to a portion of the death benefit if the insured is diagnosed with a terminal illness with 12-24 months life expectancy), guaranteed insurability rider (allows the purchase of additional coverage at specified future dates without evidence of insurability), accidental death benefit (pays an additional death benefit — typically double — if death results from an accident), and children's term rider (provides term coverage on the insured's children).

subsections.title: Life Insurance Needs Analysis Methods
paragraphs: The human life value approach calculates the present value of the insured's future earnings, adjusted for taxes, personal consumption, and inflation. For example, a 35-year-old earning $100,000 with 30 years to retirement, assuming 3% annual raises and a 5% discount rate, might have a human life value of $2.5 million. The needs approach is more practical: it identifies specific financial needs that would arise at death (final expenses, debt payoff, income replacement, education funding, emergency fund) and subtracts existing resources (savings, group life, Social Security survivor benefits, spouse's income) to determine the insurance gap.

The capital retention approach assumes the death benefit is invested and only the investment income is used, preserving the principal for the next generation. This requires a larger death benefit but provides permanent income replacement. The capital liquidation approach assumes both principal and interest are consumed over a specified period (e.g., until the youngest child reaches age 22 or the surviving spouse reaches retirement age). This requires a smaller death benefit but provides no legacy.
