chapters.title: Accounting
chapters.intro: Accounting is the language of business. Every decision — from hiring an employee to acquiring a company — ultimately shows up in the financial statements. This chapter takes you from the fundamental accounting equation through financial statement analysis, ratio interpretation, and managerial decision-making tools like CVP analysis and activity-based costing.

subsections.title: Debits & Credits
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  Opening Day at a New Business
Sarah opens a consulting firm by investing $50,000 of personal savings and taking a $30,000 bank loan. She buys $20,000 of equipment and keeps $60,000 in cash. Balance sheet: Assets = $60,000 cash + $20,000 equipment = $80,000. Liabilities = $30,000 loan. Equity = $50,000 investment. Check: $80,000 = $30,000 + $50,000. Every transaction she makes from here must keep this equation balanced.

FORMULA   Accounting Equation
Assets = Liabilities + Equity
Assets: owned items. Liabilities: debts owed. Equity: owner investment + retained earnings.
Worked Example: Assets $500K, Liabilities $300K. Equity = $200K. Borrow $100K: Assets $600K = Liabilities $400K + Equity $200K.
Common Mistake: Forgetting that EVERY transaction requires two entries to keep this equation balanced.

rows: Free Cash Flow to the Firm

subsections.title: Revenues & Expenses
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  Accrual vs. Cash: Why It Matters
A law firm completes a $50,000 case in December but the client pays in January. Under accrual accounting: $50,000 revenue in December (when earned). Under cash accounting: $50,000 revenue in January (when received). The December income statement looks completely different under each method. Most businesses use accrual because it better matches revenues with the expenses incurred to generate them.

FORMULA   Net Income
Net Income = Total Revenues - Total Expenses
Revenue: income from operations. Expenses: all costs of generating that revenue.
Worked Example: Revenue $1M, COGS $400K, SG&A $200K, Interest $50K, Tax $87.5K. NI = $262,500.
Common Mistake: Confusing cash received with revenue earned. Accrual accounting recognizes revenue when EARNED.

rows: A transaction requires at least two entries to keep the balance sheet balanced.
If there is a change in the total amount of assets, there needs to be a resulting change in liabilities, equity, or both.
Only items expressed as monetary amounts can go on a balance sheet.
A business’ finances are separate from its owner’s finances.

This is also known as the
accounting equation.

Fair value
The reasonable amount for which an item could be sold in the marketplace.
The original amount paid for an item.

Accounting assumes that an entity will operate indefinitely. Dachshund’s Doughnuts — Balance Sheet As of 31 Dec 2019 (000’s omitted) ASSETS Current Assets Cash Accounts Receivable Inventory Prepaid Expenses Total Current Assets Noncurrent Assets Property, Plant and Equip. (Less accum. depreciation) Total Noncurrent Assets $ 1,250 (330) $ 920 TOTAL
Items owned and controlled by an entity, valuable to the entity, and acquired at a measurable cost.
Goods an entity intends to sell.
Monies paid in advance for pending expenses—for example, paying rent in advance.

subsections.title: Stocks & Bonds
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  FIFO vs. LIFO in a Rising Market
A retailer bought 100 widgets at $10, then 100 more at $15. They sold 100 widgets. Under FIFO (first-in, first-out): COGS = $1,000 (sold the $10 units), ending inventory = $1,500. Higher profit, higher taxes. Under LIFO (last-in, first-out): COGS = $1,500 (sold the $15 units), ending inventory = $1,000. Lower profit, lower taxes. Same economic reality, different reported results. This is why understanding inventory methods matters for analysis.

FORMULA   Breakeven Point
Breakeven Units = Fixed Costs / (Price - Variable Cost per Unit)
Fixed costs: don't change with volume. Price - VC = Contribution Margin per unit.
Worked Example: Fixed costs $100K. Price $50. VC $30. CM = $20. Breakeven = 5,000 units.
Common Mistake: Assuming fixed costs stay fixed at all volumes. Beyond the relevant range, a second facility may be needed.

rows: Tangible assets that depreciate, or lose value, over time due to wear and tear.
Anyone who lends money or extends credit.
Debts owed to outside entities (creditors) in return for borrowed goods, services, or monies.

Obligations that won’t be paid until at least a year has passed.
The estimated amount of what will be due in taxes per year.

Money (capital) either supplied by equity investors or collected in the form of an entity’s retained earnings. subsections.title: Financial Statements
paragraphs:
FORMULA   Return on Equity
ROE = Net Income / Total Equity
Measures return generated on shareholder investment.
Worked Example: NI $500K, Equity $2M = 25% ROE. Compare to industry average ~15%: this company is generating strong returns.
Common Mistake: Comparing ROE across industries without context. Capital-intensive industries naturally have lower ROE.

rows: A liability account used when a customer has paid for a product or service, but the product or service has not yet been delivered.
The benefits of the expenditures have not yet been realized.

The benefits of the expenditures have been used up. The expenditures are now expenses. Accountants preemptively estimate how much revenue will fail to be collected, or become bad debt, by creating the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts. The Allowance for Doubtful Accounts is subtracted from Accounts Receivable at the end of the accounting period. Acco

subsections.title: Ratios
paragraphs:
FORMULA   Current Ratio
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities
Above 1.0 generally healthy. Below 1.0 = potential liquidity risk.
Worked Example: CA $800K, CL $500K = 1.6. Company has $1.60 for every $1.00 due within a year.
Common Mistake: Assuming higher is always better. Ratio of 5.0 may mean too much idle cash not being invested.

rows: Money borrowed in exchange for interest.
Borrowed money that is backed by one or more assets as collateral.
Borrowed money that is not backed with collateral.
The original amount of money that was borrowed.
A debt instrument that allows an investor to loan money for a defined period of time in exchange for regular interest payments. Repayment is made as a lump sum at the end of the period.
A bond issued by a corporation.
A bond that is issued at a deep discount to its face value and pays no interest.
A bond that can be exchanged for a specified number of common shares in the issuing company.
A bond that the issuer can pay off prior to its maturity, ending the interest payments.
Government bonds that mature in one to ten years.
Government bonds that mature in more than ten years.
The amount of money loaned to the bond issuer.
The date at which the par value is repaid to the bondholder.
The total return anticipated on a bond if the bond is held until maturity. YTM accounts for par value, market value (purchase price), coupon, current yield, and time to maturity. 15

The interest, expressed as a percentage of current market value, an investor will receive for purchasing a bond and holding it for one year. current yield = annual interest received ÷ market value = (par value × coupon) ÷ market value
The risk that the bond issuer fails to make interest payments or fails to repay the bond at maturity.
Bonds with the lowest credit risk, rated between Aaa/AAA and Baa/BBB.
Bonds with the highest credit risk, rated below Baa/ BBB. 16

subsections.title: Managerial Accounting
paragraphs:
FORMULA   Debt-to-Equity
D/E = Total Liabilities / Total Equity
Higher = more leveraged. Industry norms vary widely.
Worked Example: Liabilities $3M, Equity $2M = 1.5. Moderate leverage for manufacturing; high for services.
Common Mistake: Ignoring industry context. Banks operate at D/E of 10:1+ because their business model requires it.

rows:
THE INCOME STATEMENT

Gross and Operating Profit
Income statement
Records the revenues, expenses, and net profit of a business over a specific period of time.
The amount of money a company has earned through the sale of goods or services.
The expenses required to make goods or provide services. This includes the cost of materials and labor needed to make the good or provide the service. gross profit = revenue – COGS

The percentage of total sales revenue that the company retains after COGS has been accounted for. THE CASH FLOW STATEMENT

Cash Flows
Cash flow statement
A summary of all cash transactions during a given period.
Describes how cash has moved in and out of a company, focusing on operating, financing, and investing activities.
Include day-to-day transactions such as buying and selling inventory, paying vendors and employees, etc.
Investment in capital expenditures as well as the sale and purchase of other companies (or the stocks and bonds of other companies).

subsections.title: Excel
paragraphs:
CASE STUDY  CVP Analysis for a Product Launch Decision
A company considers launching a new product. Price: $120. Variable cost: $75. Additional fixed costs: $450,000/year. Contribution margin = $45/unit. Breakeven = 10,000 units. Market research estimates demand of 15,000 units. Projected profit: 5,000 x $45 = $225,000. But if demand is only 8,000? Loss: 2,000 x $45 = -$90,000. The CVP analysis shows management needs confidence that demand exceeds 10,000 before greenlighting. Sensitivity: what price increase would be needed to break even at 8,000 units? ($450,000/8,000) + $75 = $131.25 — an 9.4% price increase.

rows: Activities between the company and its investors and lenders. It is a cash inflow when money is received from investors but an outflow when cash is paid to investors. ACCOUNTING RATIOS

Liquidity and Solvency
Liquidity

Methods of Assigning Costs
Job order costing
Assigns costs to individual units of inventory. Each piece of inventory may vary in cost.

The more inventory produced, the higher the variable cost. Fixed costs remain constant regardless of production. 23

Introducing Microeconomics

Supply and Demand

subsections.title: Connections to Other Disciplines
paragraphs:
KEY INSIGHT  Finance
Financial statements are the inputs to valuation models. Every DCF analysis starts with the income statement and cash flow statement you learned to build here.

KEY INSIGHT  Strategy
Ratio analysis is used for competitive benchmarking. Comparing your ratios to competitors reveals strategic strengths and weaknesses.

KEY INSIGHT  Marketing & Pricing
CVP analysis from managerial accounting directly informs pricing decisions. Your breakeven point determines the minimum viable price.

subsections.title: Financial Services Applications
paragraphs:
FINANCIAL SERVICES  Insurance Carrier Financial Analysis
Statutory Accounting Principles (SAP) used by state regulators are more conservative than GAAP — assets valued lower, liabilities recognized earlier. When evaluating a carrier like National Life Group (AM Best A+, $57.4B AUM), read the statutory annual statement for surplus position, Risk-Based Capital (RBC) ratio (above 300% = strong), loss ratios, and investment portfolio composition. Understanding carrier financials lets you confidently recommend products backed by strong balance sheets.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Commission and Override Accounting
First-year commissions on IUL products typically run 80-115% of target premium. Renewal commissions of 2-5% in years 2-10. As a manager, override income on team production represents 10-25% of their first-year commissions. Revenue recognition: commission income is earned when the policy is issued and first premium paid — not when the application is submitted. This distinction affects cash flow planning significantly.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Tax Treatment of Insurance Products
IRC Section 7702 defines life insurance for tax purposes. Benefits: tax-free death benefits (Section 101(a)), tax-deferred cash value growth, tax-free policy loans (if not a Modified Endowment Contract). The MEC test (7-pay test) limits how much premium can be paid in the first 7 years. 1035 exchanges allow tax-free transfers between qualifying policies. Understanding these rules is essential for designing client solutions and explaining policy benefits accurately.

MASTERY CHECK  Accounting Mastery Check
Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Accounting concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.

MARKETS & ECONOMIES
Supply & Demand  |  Economic Worldview  |  Markets & Externalities  |  Economic Fluctuations  |  Fiscal & Monetary Policy  |  International Trade

chapters.title: Markets & Economies
chapters.intro: Economics provides the framework for understanding how markets work, why prices change, what drives economic growth, and how government policy shapes the business environment. This chapter covers microeconomics (individual markets and decision-making) and macroeconomics (the economy as a whole, including growth, inflation, unemployment, and policy).

subsections.title: Supply & Demand
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  How a Supply Shock Cascades Through Markets
A drought destroys 30% of Brazil's coffee crop. Supply curve shifts left (less available at every price). At the old price, shortage emerges. Price rises to new equilibrium — higher price, lower quantity. Coffee shops face higher input costs. Some raise prices (passing cost to consumers). Others absorb it (lower margins). Some switch to tea offerings (substitution). This is exactly what happened in 2021 when frost damage caused a 50% price spike. Understanding supply/demand shifts lets you anticipate price movements in any market.

FORMULA   Price Elasticity of Demand
Ed = (% Change in Quantity Demanded) / (% Change in Price)
|Ed| > 1 = elastic. |Ed| < 1 = inelastic. |Ed| = 1 = unit elastic.
Worked Example: Gym raises price 20%. Members drop 15%. Ed = -0.75 (inelastic). Revenue increases with price increase because quantity drops proportionally less.
Common Mistake: Forgetting the revenue implication: elastic goods — price increase DECREASES revenue. Inelastic goods — price increase INCREASES revenue.

rows: The summation of all of the individual supplies of a good or service.
to the left—market supply has decreased.
Shows how the total quantity supplied of a good changes as its price changes. $8 $7 Pablo’s New SC $6 $5 $4 to the right—market supply has increased. 100 200 300 400 500 600
The quantity where equilibrium occurs (400 on the chart).
The point at which the price and quantity demanded is equal to the price and quantity supplied.
When there is a low price for goods or services, consumers buy more and sellers supply less. $10

The price where equilibrium occurs ($9 on the chart). $9 Demand Curve Supply Curve $8 $7 100 200 300 400 500 600 700 Market Supply of Bacon Shakes When there is a high price for goods or services, consumers buy less and sellers supply more.

The price of any good will naturally adjust until market equilibrium is reached. Supply > demand: There is a surplus. Prices will drop until equilibrium is met. Demand > supply: There is a shortage. Prices will rise until equilibrium is met. Supply = demand: The market has reached equilibrium. nd Which is greater? a em D Price Increases Demand Decr
To recognize events that alter equilibrium
1. Identify a shift in the DC and/or the SC. 2. Determine if the curve(s) shift left or right. 3. Use a graph to see how the shifts change the EP. 31
The satisfaction gained from consuming a good or service. A consumer’s total utility can be positive or negative.
Theoretical units used to measure satisfaction.
Utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good.

% Change in P % Change in Q P2 — P1 P2 + P1 2 Q2 — Q1 Q2 + Q1 2 Sellers often use Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) to determine the potential loss of customers if prices are raised. Price Elasticity of Demand (PED) % Change in Q % Change in P
When the percentage change in quantity demanded is equal to that in price.

subsections.title: Economic Worldview
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  Why Insurance Is Inelastic
Price elasticity for term life insurance is approximately -0.3 (inelastic). A 10% premium increase causes only a 3% drop in policies sold. Why? Because insurance is a necessity for people with dependents, and switching costs are high (new underwriting, potential health changes). This means carriers can raise premiums with relatively small customer losses. For advisors, it means clients are more sensitive to perceived VALUE than to price — focus conversations on coverage adequacy, not premium shopping.

C: consumer spending. I: business investment. G: government spending. X: exports. M: imports.
Worked Example: US GDP ~$28 trillion. Consumer spending is ~68% ($19T). Government ~17%. Investment ~18%. Net exports slightly negative (trade deficit).
Common Mistake: Confusing GDP growth with wellbeing. GDP measures economic output, not distribution, sustainability, or quality of life.

subsections.title: International Trade
paragraphs:
CASE STUDY  Adverse Selection in Health Insurance
The individual health insurance market exhibits classic adverse selection: people likely to need healthcare are more likely to buy coverage. Healthy people opt out, making the insured pool sicker, driving premiums higher, causing more healthy people to exit — a death spiral. The ACA addressed this with coverage mandates (forcing healthy people in) and premium subsidies. In life insurance, adverse selection is managed through underwriting: medical exams, health questionnaires, and risk classification ensure pricing reflects actual risk. Understanding market failures isn't just theory — it's the foundation of insurance product design.

subsections.title: Connections to Other Disciplines
paragraphs:
KEY INSIGHT  Finance
Interest rates drive the cost of capital. When the Fed changes rates, WACC changes for every company — affecting investment decisions you'll model in Finance.

KEY INSIGHT  Marketing & Pricing
Price elasticity from economics directly informs pricing strategy. Inelastic demand = pricing power. Elastic demand = compete on value, not price.

KEY INSIGHT  Strategy
PESTEL analysis (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal) uses macroeconomic understanding to assess the external strategic environment.

subsections.title: Financial Services Applications
paragraphs:
FINANCIAL SERVICES  Interest Rate Impact on Insurance Products
The Federal Reserve's rate decisions cascade through financial services: Fed funds rate (3.50-3.75% as of March 2026) affects Treasury yields, which affect insurance company general account investment returns, which affect IUL cap rates and FIA crediting rates. When rates rise, new IUL policies generally offer better cap rates (good for sales). When rates fall, existing policies may underperform illustrations. Understanding this chain lets you time product recommendations and set realistic client expectations.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Arizona Market Demographics
Pima County (Tucson metro, ~1M population, University of Arizona anchor, significant retiree market). Mohave County (Lake Havasu/Kingman, ~220K, retirement and second-home market). Santa Cruz County (~47K, border economy, underserved for financial services). The $84 trillion great wealth transfer over 20 years creates sustained demand. With the estate tax exemption permanently at $15M/individual (OBBBA, July 2025), planning focus shifts from tax avoidance to income tax optimization, asset protection, and legacy planning.

MASTERY CHECK  Markets & Economies Mastery Check
Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Markets & Economies concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.

FINANCE
Time Value of Money  |  Financial Management  |  Capital Structure  |  Modigliani-Miller  |  Capital Budgeting  |  Value Creation  |  Excel

chapters.title: Finance
chapters.intro: Finance answers the fundamental question: how do we make decisions about money across time? From evaluating whether a project is worth investing in, to determining how a company should fund itself, to valuing an entire business — finance provides the quantitative tools for value-based decision-making. The time value of money is the foundation: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.

subsections.title: Time Value of Money
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  The Power of Compounding
Invest $10,000 at 8% annually. After 10 years: $21,589. After 20 years: $46,610. After 30 years: $100,627. Your original $10,000 grew 10x — but $90,627 of that growth came from returns on returns (compound interest), not your original investment. At 12%, the 30-year value jumps to $299,599. The 4% difference in return rate produces a 3x difference in outcome over 30 years. This is why even small differences in investment returns matter enormously — and why financial planning starts as early as possible.

FORMULA   Future Value
FV = PV x (1 + r)^n
PV: present value. r: rate per period. n: number of periods.
Worked Example: $10,000 at 8% for 10 years: FV = $10,000 x 1.08^10 = $21,589.
Common Mistake: Not matching rate to compounding period. Annual 8% with monthly compounding: use r=0.08/12, n=120.

subsections.title: Financial Management
paragraphs:
SCENARIO  NPV vs. IRR: When They Disagree
Project A costs $50K, returns $80K in year 1. IRR = 60%, NPV at 10% = $22.7K. Project B costs $500K, returns $700K in year 1. IRR = 40%, NPV at 10% = $136.4K. IRR says choose A (60% > 40%). NPV says choose B ($136.4K > $22.7K). Who is right? NPV. Project B creates 6x more value despite a lower percentage return. IRR measures efficiency; NPV measures value creation. When they conflict (different project sizes, different timing), ALWAYS follow NPV.

FORMULA   Present Value
PV = FV / (1 + r)^n
FV: future amount. r: discount rate. n: periods.
Worked Example: $50,000 in 5 years at 10%: PV = $50,000/1.10^5 = $31,046. Worth paying up to $31,046 today.
Common Mistake: Using wrong discount rate. Rate must reflect risk: government bond = risk-free; startup = much higher.

subsections.title: Modigliani-Miller
paragraphs:
FORMULA   WACC
WACC = (E/V x Ke) + (D/V x Kd x (1-T))
E: equity. D: debt. V: E+D. Ke: cost of equity. Kd: cost of debt. T: tax rate.
Worked Example: E=$60M, D=$40M, Ke=12%, Kd=6%, T=30%. WACC = (0.6x12%) + (0.4x6%x0.7) = 7.2% + 1.68% = 8.88%.
Common Mistake: Forgetting to use AFTER-TAX cost of debt. Interest is tax-deductible, so 6% debt at 30% tax = 4.2% true cost.

Companies split cash flows into two categories

There are many ways a company can drive growth, including

• Network effects: When additional users add value to a product or business. • Value innovation: The simultaneous pursuit of radically superior value for buyers and lower costs for companies. • Bolt-on acquisition: The acquisition of a small company that can be easily integrated into the parent.
Owners can add value to a company in several ways
• Connections with other businesses • Good management or governance • Access to key resources

Value is calculated as the present value of a company's future cash flows. The riskier the business, the higher the cost of capital, generally speaking. Calculating Investment Rate Investment = rate growth rate

paragraphs:
FORMULA   CAPM (Cost of Equity)
Ke = Rf + Beta x (Rm - Rf)
Rf: risk-free rate. Beta: stock volatility vs. market. Rm-Rf: market risk premium.
Worked Example: Rf=3%, Beta=1.2, Market premium=7%. Ke = 3% + 1.2x7% = 11.4%.
Common Mistake: Using historical beta for a company whose risk profile has changed significantly.

Annuities
Annuity

A fixed-sum payment typically made every year or at some other period (e.g., monthly). • An annuity in which payments are made at the beginning of each period is an annuity due. • An annuity in which payments are made at the end of each period is an ordinary annuity.

The Excel function that calculates future values. How does FV work? • FV takes the arguments rate, number of periods, regular payment, present value, and type (optional). • With the type argument set to 1, Excel adds the regular payment then compounds. All regular payments are compounded.

When using the FV and PV functions, pay close attention to positive (inflows) and negative (outflows) cash flows. • With the type argument set to 0 or omitted, Excel compounds, then adds the regular payment. The last payment is not compounded.

The Excel function that calculates the internal rate of return. How does IRR work? • IRR takes the arguments values and guess (optional). The values are the cash inflows and outflows each year of a timeline. • Do not use present values in the IRR function.

Culture

Setting goals will help you improve your cultural intelligence

• Metacognitive: An overall awareness of how culture affects interactions and an ability to strategize accordingly. • Frustration • Behavioral: The ability to mimic another culture to help others interpret you correctly. • Honeymoon • Adjustment • Acceptance • Motivational: The emotional resilience and desire to adapt to another culture.

subsections.title: Value Creation
paragraphs:
CASE STUDY  Regional Expansion Capital Budgeting
A financial services firm evaluates two growth strategies. Option A: Hire 5 advisors at $30K each ($150K total), expecting $400K first-year production generating $80K override income, growing 15% annually. Option B: Invest $150K in digital marketing, expecting $200K leads generating $60K revenue year 1, growing 25% annually. At 12% discount rate, 5-year NPV of A = $127K, NPV of B = $148K. Despite A having higher first-year returns, B creates more total value due to higher growth rate. This demonstrates why NPV — not gut feel or first-year returns — should drive capital allocation.

paragraphs:
KEY INSIGHT  Accounting
Financial statements from Accounting are the inputs to every valuation. DCF starts with the cash flow statement; multiples analysis uses income statement data.

KEY INSIGHT  Strategy
Capital budgeting is how strategy gets funded. Porter's frameworks identify WHICH opportunities to pursue; NPV determines WHETHER they create value.

KEY INSIGHT  Data & Decisions
Probability distributions from Data & Decisions power scenario analysis in capital budgeting. Monte Carlo simulation uses probability to model investment uncertainty.

subsections.title: Financial Services Applications
paragraphs:
FINANCIAL SERVICES  Premium Financing NPV Analysis
Client needs $5M death benefit. Annual premium $100K for 10 years. Loan rate: SOFR (3.65%) + 250bps = 6.15%. Policy crediting rate: 7.5% (1.35% spread). Year 1: loan balance $106,150, cash value ~$85,000. Year 5: loan ~$585K, cash value ~$475K. Year 10: loan ~$1.06M, cash value ~$1.15M (crossover). At death: $5M benefit minus loan payoff = net benefit. Stress test: at 5.5% crediting, crossover extends to year 15+. This is why running multiple scenarios is non-negotiable in premium financing.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Agent Recruiting as Capital Budgeting
Cost per recruit: $15K-30K (licensing, training, marketing support, time). Expected outcome: 35% probability agent reaches $100K production in 18 months. At 20% override: $20K annual return on $25K investment = 80% ROI if successful. Expected value: 35% x $20K = $7K expected return on $25K = 28% expected ROI. Still attractive but very different from headline 80%. This is portfolio thinking: recruit enough agents to let the law of large numbers work in your favor.

MASTERY CHECK  Finance Mastery Check
Can you: (1) Define the 10 most important terms in this chapter without looking? (2) Apply each major framework to a business scenario you haven't seen before? (3) Work through the key formulas with different numbers? (4) Explain how Finance concepts connect to at least 2 other disciplines in this manual? (5) Describe how these concepts apply in financial services practice? If yes on all five, you have mastery.

chapters.title: Leading Organizations
chapters.intro: Leadership determines whether strategy gets executed. This chapter covers cultural intelligence for diverse environments, team dynamics and group decision-making, organizational design, corporate governance, leadership theories, business ethics, and talent management. These are the people skills that differentiate managers who merely supervise from leaders who inspire.

Components and Characteristics of Teams

Organizational identity

A set of shared values and beliefs that underline a company’s identity and guide employee behavior. It provides members with an idea of what the company represents, and helps them understand their role within the organization. artifacts values basic assumptions

• Basic assumptions: Organizational values that have become entrenched. Employees take them for granted and are unaware of them. • Values: Professed, enduring beliefs that serve as a company’s behavioral compass. * Espoused values are communicated by management. * Enacted values are what employees actually do. • Artifacts: Visible, tangible element

One of the most influential frameworks used to categorize culture, it proposes that organizations can be assessed along two axes: direction of focus and degree of control. Stability/Control • Market cultures deliver results by any means necessary. • Hierarchy cultures favor formalization, standardizing, and optimizing internal processes.

It proposes that organizational culture is represented by seven values: • Stable cultures are highly structured to produce constant levels of output. • Innovative cultures optimize adaptability and encourage risk-taking.

The Trait Approach to Leadership
Trait approach to leadership
A theory that focuses on the traits that leaders generally exhibit.

• Intelligence: The ability to learn information and apply it to life tasks. • Emotional intelligence: The ability to control one's own emotions and understand other people's emotions. • Self-confidence: Self-confident leaders are more assertive and secure. • Integrity: The quality of trustworthiness.

To diagnose problems using the action wheel, ask two fundamental questions: 1. What is really going on? 2. What are we going to do about it? Five dimensions of authentic leaders are purpose, values, relationships, self-discipline, and heart.

ETHICAL PRACTICES IN BUSINESS

Introducing Business Ethics
Ethics

A system of socially-influenced norms and rules that guide decision making, usually in a specific context. Business ethics is more specific; it refers to the codes of conduct that guide the actions of people engaged in business activity.

An assumption in the field of business ethics that the corporation is an individual actor. Ethics are different from morals, which are more about an individual’s personal philosophy of right and wrong.
Your ethical decisions will rarely—if ever—be straightforward!
Supports and promotes ethical decision making, and attracts ethical employees.

The way a company directs its actions, and the extent to which its actions are governed by codes of conduct, company values, and the law. A code of conduct outlines the rules and values that guide a company’s ethics.
A company’s divisions, their relationships, and the reporting processes between them.

Judge whether conduct is ethical based on the conduct’s consequences. • Common good approach: Actions and decisions that benefit the community in question are deemed ethical. • Utilitarian approach: Actions and decisions that do the most good for the most people are ethical.

Judge whether conduct is ethical based upon its intrinsic properties. • Rights approach: Actions and decisions that infringe on the rights of another are unethical. • Virtue approach: Focuses on the character of the people taking action. Desirable characteristics include integrity, courage, generosity, prudence, and more. • Justice approach: Equals
A department manager should receive more compensation than staff, but less than the CEO, and equal to that of a fellow department manager.
Identifying the individuals or groups who will be affected by any decision.

taking action

Does this situation have an ethical component, and if so, how important is it? Ethical issues Conflicts of interest, bribes, issues involving honesty and integrity. Non-issues Adhering to good company policy, justified discipline of an employee.

Argues that a corporation’s primary duty is to maximize shareholder profits. • Classical model: Focuses on profits over all else. • Moral minimum model: Also has a primary focus on profits, but adds that companies should minimize harm. • Both the classical model and moral minimum model stress the importance of obeying the law.

chapters.title: Strategy
chapters.intro: Strategy answers: how does a firm achieve and sustain competitive advantage? This chapter covers business-level strategy (how to compete within an industry using Porter's generic strategies), corporate-level strategy (which industries to compete in through diversification and vertical integration), international expansion, strategic frameworks (PESTEL, VRIO, Value Chain), implementation through the Balanced Scorecard, and strategic decision-making under uncertainty.

Global Business Environment
International strategy
The theory of how to gain competitive advantage by operating across national borders.
The worldwide trend of economic integration across borders. Improvements in technology and elimination of trade barriers have resulted in an increase in globalization.

Physical distance, climate difference, and number of communicative/transportation links.

Modifying business models to improve the local responsiveness of foreign markets.
Focusing on similarities between regions to scale up production and decrease costs across a standardized organization, generating economies of scale or scope.

IMPLEMENTING YOUR STRATEGY

Cascading a Strategic Plan
Strategy implementation

The process of turning a strategic plan into action with the intent of improving business outcomes. Strategy implementation involves: Strategy implementation is also known as strategy execution. 1. Drawing up a strategic plan. 2. Using operations to cascade the plan across the entire organization.
The process by which an organization’s overarching strategy is conveyed to the entire organization.
There are several steps in the process, including

• Identify each part of the organization that needs to be aligned with the new strategic plan. • Make the strategic plan available to managers from every business unit. • Align support units by ensuring that managers understand the strategic plan and the business units’ objectives. Managers can then identify how their departments can help. Tips for

Groups of activities and routines that are essential to the execution of the strategic plan. Dashboards are a useful way to collect feedback on KPI performance. Project reviews
Daily or weekly check-ins between managers and employees.
Meetings held by individual departments to monitor dashboard data and see how the business is running over the short term.
Quarterly, high-level meetings held to review the strategic objectives within each theme in the strategic plan.

Making Decisions and Understanding Bias
Decision
A choice made between alternate courses of action in a situation of uncertainty.

The Decision Making Process

Decision-making process
A systematic approach to making decisions.

Even if we do an excellent job of collecting relevant information before a decision, we may still misinterpret the data and make bad decisions. • Survivorship bias: Ignoring past failure in favor of a success narrative. • Availability bias: Overvaluing facts that come readily to mind.

A protocol that mitigates cognitive bias by standardizing evaluation criteria and evaluating data only after all assessments are complete. MAP Process Determine assessments in advance Complete assessments separately Make a final evaluation • Assumption-based planning (ABP) forces participants to name their assumptions and make a plan for how to cha

Decisions that you should empower others to make in order to drive decision-making as far down in the organization as possible.
A decision should be delegated if it’s reversible and the person making it can be held accountable.
A planning and management tool that helps businesses develop, implement, and track a well-rounded strategy.

chapters.title: Data & Decisions
chapters.intro: Data literacy separates good decisions from lucky ones. This chapter covers descriptive statistics (summarizing data), probability (quantifying uncertainty), probability distributions (modeling random variables), regression analysis (predicting outcomes from variables), data collection methods, and statistical inference (drawing conclusions from samples and testing hypotheses).

Expected value, E(X)

A measure of how spread out a distribution is from its mean. A distribution with high variance is more spread out, while one with low variance is narrower. Standard deviation is the square root of the variance.

A measure of the symmetry of a distribution. A good rule of thumb is that a skewness above 1 or below -1 is highly asymmetrical. Variance is always positive, while skewness can be positive or negative to indicate the direction of the skew.
A value that relates a probability distribution family to a specific situation. It tells us how to tweak the distribution to properly model this situation. 1 0.9 0.8 0.7

Normal distribution

A commonly used continuous probability distribution known for its bell-shaped, unimodal, symmetric curve. Often used to model natural population phenomena like heights, lengths, and weights. 𝜇 The mean, μ, and the standard deviation, σ, are the two parameters that define a normal curve. 𝜎

The distribution of the means of many samples from a population will be roughly normally distributed, even if the underlying population distribution is not normal.

If a data set is normally distributed, 68% of the data will fall within one standard deviation from the mean. Also called the 68-95-99.7 rule, since 95% of the data falls within two standard deviations of the mean, and 99.7% of the data falls within three. Correlation and Causation
Correlation
A relationship that exists when one event can be used to predict a second event; if A correlates to B, B correlates to A.

In an experiment, the test subjects that do not receive the treatment under investigation.
The test subjects in an experiment who do receive the treatment under investigation.
The variable that is manipulated in the experiment. In a perfect experiment, the independent variable is the only difference between the treatment group and the control group.

The variable being measured in an experiment—specifically, how it is affected or not affected by a change in the independent variable. The placebo effect is a confounding variable that occurs when the thought of being tested or treated causes a reaction from the subject. 400

Residual analysis
Investigating error values and outliers in a model to assess the model’s accuracy.
A graph with residual values on the y-axis and the independent variable or the model’s predicted values on the x-axis. A good model should have a randomly scattered residual plot.
Performing a mathematical operation on every data point in a data set that linearizes the relationship between this input and the output of a multivariate linear model.
• Take the inverse of each observation. • Take the square root of each observation. • Square each observation.

Variable Selection
Backward elimination
A variable selection method that starts with all of the variables in consideration, then removes them one by one.

A variable selection method that starts with no variables and adds them one by one. Adjusted R2: A value similar to R2 that assesses a multivariate model’s accuracy, but punishes a model for having too many variables relative to their benefit. p-value: The probability that a variable is not significant in a model. A good variable usually has a p-va
Overfitting: A model has so many input variables that it describes randomness rather than real relationships.
Two of the independent variables in a model are correlated, which muddles a model’s accuracy.
A variable that isn’t included in a model, but affects both independent and dependent variables.
A prediction made using an input that lies within the set of observed values. These are usually reliable predictions.

The movement of time series data as it responds to the broad and unpredictable economic cycle of recession and growth.
The predictable, repetitive rise and fall of timebased outcomes.
A time series analysis method in which the independent variable is a previous outcome, rather than time.
How much a stock jumps up and down compared to the broader market. A high beta means high volatility.

A collection of data from every member in a population.
A subgroup taken from the population.
A list or database of all members of a population.
Every element in a population has an equal chance of being selected for a sample.
Every element in a population does not have an equal chance of being selected for a sample.

Simple and Stratified Random Sampling
Simple random sampling
1. Create a sampling frame. 2. Randomly select elments from the frame to be in the sample.

Cluster and Systematic Sampling
Cluster random sampling

1. Divide a population into clusters: groups that are representative of the whole population. Cluster random sampling saves money. 2. Use random sampling to select a sample of clusters. 3. Survey each element within those clusters.
1. Divide the size of the frame (N) by the desired sample size (n) to get the index number (k). 2. Starting with a randomly selected number, choose every kth element in the frame.
If N = 500 and n = 50, we would choose every 10th item from the frame.
A population is divided into groups and then uses convenience sampling to fill a quota of required samples.
When an interviewer asks if a subject can provide friends to be interviewed.
A method of data collection where individuals are asked questions in order to measure ideas, opinions, and experiences.
People with strong opinions are more likely to respond to a poll.
When a survey receives little to no response.
When people respond with a knowingly false answer.

CONFIDENCE INTERVALS

Working with Confidence Intervals
Parameters
Numerical facts that describe an entire population. point estimate
Values gleaned from a random sample of a population. margin of error
The process of using data from a sample to draw conclusions about an entire population.

A percentage that specifies how confident one is that a confidence interval contains the parameter of interest. A confidence level of 95% says that, for every 100 samples taken, 95 of the resulting confidence intervals will likely contain the parameter. In a confidence interval for means, the point estimate is the sample mean, x, which is used to e
Sampling distribution for the sample mean
A hypothetical plot of sample means vs. their frequency in an infinite number of repeat samples of the same size. This plot is centered around the true population mean.
We calculate the margin of error using the critical value

a Z-score that corresponds to our confidence level and maps to the sampling distribution. For 95% confidence, Z = 1.96. In a confidence interval for proportions, the point estimate is p̂, the sample proportion, which is used to estimate the population proportion, p.

Frequency in repeat samples

Confidence interval

A range of values computed from a sample statistic that is highly likely to contain the population parameter. A confidence interval is centered around a point estimate (a single sample statistic), and half the width is the margin of error.  50 52 54 56 58 60 62 64 Sample mean x (in points)   66    p̂ = point estimate Z = critical value n = sample s

HYPOTHESIS TESTING

Setting Up The Hypothesis
Hypothesis testing
A branch of inferential statistics that allows us to use samples to draw conclusions about a population based on a set of statistical hypotheses.
A testable assumption about a population parameter. The first step in hypothesis testing is to set the following two hypotheses:

A safe or commonly held assumption, written H₀. If there is sufficient evidence that H₀ is false, the researcher can reject H₀. If there is insufficient evidence that H₀ is false, the researcher will fail to reject H₀.

A proposal that’s outside of what is safe or expected, written Hα. The alternative hypothesis states that the population parameter is somehow different from a given value. The null hypothesis typically contains an equals sign. H₀: µ = ? or p = ? The alternative hypothesis typically contains ≠, > or <. Hα: µ ≠ ?, µ < ?, or µ > ? Hα : p ≠ ?, p < ?, o

Two-tailed
Rejection
regions

Assumption of Normality

In a hypothesis test, we assume that the sampling distribution is normal and centered around the true population parameter. This is usually a sound assumption with a large and sufficiently random sample.
The tail(s) of a sampling distribution, the area under which corresponds with the significance level α. If a result falls in a rejection region, we have sufficient evidence to reject H0.
A test with a rejection region on only one side of the sampling distribution, typically characterized by a < or > sign in the alternative hypothesis.

ONE- AND TWO-TAILED TESTS

p-Values
p-value
A statistical measure that gives the exact probability of achieving a result that’s as or more extreme than the result when H0 is true.
When the result of a hypothesis test meets level, but has little practical significance.

The practice of only using data that supports a desired conclusion. p-hacking: The process of manipulating data collection or analysis until non-significant results become significant. This includes: • Deciding mid-test whether to keep collecting data • Trying out several analyses until significance is achieved • Gathering data on many variables in
Reporting insignificant results as if they’re nearly significant. An inconclusive result does not meet α level, while a statistically significant result does meet level.

Z-statistic formula for a
hypothesis test for means

Confidence interval: A range of values used to estimate a population parameter with a certain level of confidence. Confident intervals are widely used in statistics to communicate the prevision and reliability of statistical estimates. The sample mean, x̄ , is the point estimate of the population μ.
DATA AND DECISIONS — STATISTICAL INFERENCE: MAKING DATA-DRIVEN DECISIONS
You can actually use a confidence interval as part of (or in lieu of) a two-tailed hypothesis test by checking if μ falls within the confidence interval. Margin of Error = this distance 95%

Each benefit stems from a specific feature of the product.
Perceived value

The value people believe they will receive from a product. A product can be a tangible good, such as a pair of shoes, a service (e.g., a doctor’s check-up), or intellectual property (e.g., song lyrics).

A plan of what to sell, whom to sell it to, and how to sell it that is focused on long-term profit, rather than short-term gains. Conducting a Situation Analysis A situation analysis consists of two tasks. 1. Examining the four Cs: company, competitors, customers, and collaborators.

An evaluation of a company’s resources and capabilities, its competitors, and general market demand. SWOT Analysis POSITIVE NEGATIVE Strengths Weaknesses CURRENT ATTRIBUTES Opportunities Threats POTENTIAL CONDITIONS 2. Interpreting the information gathered in the four Cs within the framework of a SWOT analysis, which describes the company’s strengt

A statement describing the core benefits that a product offers, used internally to guide product messaging.

Value Propositions
The value proposition (VP) is
1. The benefits a product provides to the target audience. 2. A statement describing those benefits.

A type of value proposition statement that communicates the essence of what makes a product unique to the target audience. A benefit is the practical and emotional advantages or gains that a product provides to target customers. A feature is a distinctive attribute or aspect of a product or service that provides a specific benefit to the target cus

MARKETING IN PRACTICE

The Purchase Funnel
Purchase funnel
The process potential customers (prospects) go through before they decide to buy a product.

Marketing activities should be tailored to each stage of the
funnel

• Awareness: Use educational and interesting content that introduces your company and its products. Strive for high reach—exposing many people to your messaging—among those you want to sell to, i.e., your target market. • Interest: Answer prospects’ questions and provide useful information via product sheets, testimonials, case studies, etc. • Desi
The quantity of each type of a product consumers and companies will buy in a given period of time.
The percentage of total units sold for a given product relative to the entire market.
A set of products that are closely linked by function, target market, price range, or distribution channel.
All of the product lines and individual products a company offers.

The specific characteristics or qualities of a product that distinguish it from competitors and meet the needs of the target audience. Branding—which includes names, symbols, and other design elements—creates a unique identity for the product that customers can identify and attach meaning to.
The chain of businesses and individuals that get products to customers.
The product goes directly from producer to customer.
Buys one type of product directly from different producers and resells primarily to wholesalers.

Buys products in bulk from distributors and resells them in smaller amounts to retailers. Producer Retailers purchase products from other intermediaries, stock them, and sell them directly to the end customer. Creating a distribution strategy involves choosing a distribution channel and distribution intensity—i.e., the level of a product’s availabi
When companies promote mentions from influential individuals.

Temporary offers that incentivize customers to buy. • Earned: Generated by a third party. • Paid: Displayed outside company channels. Ads that appear on the web are considered online advertising or digital advertising, whereas traditional media is offline advertising.

BRANDING FUNDAMENTALS

Brand Building and Brand Value
Brand
A name, term, symbol, or other feature that identifies a product and distinguishes it from its competitors.
How customers actually feel about a brand.
The process of creating and maintaining a brand. What can be Branded?
The extent to which consumers can correctly identify a brand when prompted by a visual or verbal cue.
The extent to which consumers can correctly identify a brand from memory, without cues.
Dedication to a brand indicated by a positive attitude and/or repeat purchase behavior.

Things like sounds, taglines, symbols, and more that help develop a brand identity. Building a Brand's Personality Symbols, colors, taglines, sounds, rhythms, and names are all used by companies to build a unique brand personality, or traits that customers attribute to a brand. • Tagline: A short, catchy phrase for communicating a brand to consumer
The emotions, features, places, and other things that people associate with brands.

The sum of all positive and negative attributes that determine how valuable the brand's name and symbol seem to customers. It's four dimensions include: Factors in Brand Equity 1. Brand awareness, or how familiar customers are with a brand 2. Brand loyalty, or how dedicated customers are to a brand 3. Brand association, or what customers associate
How a brand wants to be perceived by customers.
In total, your brand essence will
• Encapsulate why customers care about your brand

chapters.title: Marketing & Pricing
chapters.intro: Marketing creates, communicates, and delivers perceived value. Pricing captures that value as revenue. Together, they drive customer acquisition, retention, and profitability. This chapter covers the marketing mix (4Ps), segmentation-targeting-positioning (STP), brand management, digital marketing funnels, A/B testing, and pricing strategies from cost-plus through dynamic pricing and auction mechanisms.

BUILDING YOUR BRAND

Positioning and Mapping
Perceptual mapping
Plotting brands on axes, each of which represents an attribute that drives brand selection. A map is informed by Aaker's brand personality framework:
Once a brand's personality is scored, a perceptual map can be developed, like this one on sophistication and ruggedness:
Your target consumer's reason to buy.
How customers think and feel about your product in relation to competitors' products.
A short description of the target market and a how you intend this market to see your brand.
The percentage of consumers who are aware of a brand.
A brand's monetary value, or ability to generate revenue. • Brand equity can be assessed by computing the difference in price consumers are willing to pay for a brand vs. competitors.
A measurement of brand performance based on running a survey before a marketing campaign and after it, and comparing the results.
• Do research and decide what to test • Formulate a hypothesis • Design the test and select the right sample • Collect data • Analyze test results

Posits that there is a significant difference between the two groups being compared. Don’t change multiple variables in an A/B test.
Says there’s no significant difference between the two groups being compared.

Designing and Conducting an A/B Test
Control
The group that receives the original version.
The group that receives the modified version.
A group of individuals that have similar characteristics to a tester’s target population.

An issue with the implementation of your testing tools or code, usually due to user error.

The sample you’re using isn’t representative of the typical population.
Grab consumers’ attention where they look for information and familiarize them with your product or service.
Display knowledge and possible solutions by providing useful and interesting content.
Offer detailed information and solutions through product sheets, case studies, webinars, etc.

Interest

In addition to the ultimate goal of sales, marketing goals
include
Awareness: Getting prospects to (1) know you exist, (2) know you provide a specific product or service, and (3) think of you when they need that product or service.
Keeping prospects interested through valuable content until they’re ready to buy. B2B (business-to-business) marketing targets companies, while B2C (business-toconsumer) targets individual consumers.
Research-based representations of who buyers are, and when, where, how, and why they buy.

The cost per 1,000 ad impressions. Advertiser Spends money to have its ads shown Publisher Takes money to place ads on its website, app, etc. 2. Cost-per-click (CPC): You pay for an ad only when it’s clicked on.
Each time an ad loads on an individual’s browser 3. Cost-per-action (CPA): The cost per completion of a predetermined action (a.k.a. conversion), such as a purchase.
Each time an individual clicks on an ad
Affiliate sites earn a commission for each conversion they generate for an advertiser. Often uses CPA pricing.
Each time an action is taken on the buyer’s journey

Digital Marketing Metrics
Key performance indicators (KPIs)
The metrics used to evaluate a campaign, ideally chosen based on the campaign’s goals.
Return on advertising spend (ROAS): Revenue generated from an ad campaign divided by the campaign’s cost. cost-per- cost-per-click (CPC) action = conversion rate (CPA)
The ratio of conversions to site visits.
Using tactics, including personalization and reducing page load time, to raise conversion rates.

Cost divided by the number of conversions. Targeting Targeting in paid digital advertising allows advertisers to reach very specific audiences based, e.g., on their age or location. Retargeting (a.k.a. remarketing) reaches people who have already interacted with a company and thus likely have some interest in it. Pixel-based retargeting reaches peo
Content from third parties, such as journalists.
Any form of advertising on websites.

When ads are run prominently and exclusively on a whole webpage. Takeovers usually last at least 24 hours. 2. Video

A company that aggregates advertising space from multiple publishers and sells it to advertisers. Ads may run on specifically chosen sites in a network or run-of-network (RON). 3. Rich media: Interactive ads that respond to users’ actions

Ads that can only be seen by scrolling down. Search Marketing Search marketing—promoting a website by increasing its visibility in search engines—is effective because people are usually receptive to information about something they’re looking for. •

Build a user-friendly website.

Paid ads in search listings. Paid search ads appear in search results when a user’s search query—the text they enter into a search engine— includes a keyword or key phrase chosen by the advertiser. number of emails opened ÷ number of emails delivered.

Increasing the desirability of a product by augmenting the product offer without changing the product. A company’s marketing strategy—a plan of what to sell, whom to sell it to, and how to sell it, aimed at maximizing long-term profit—sets limits on its pricing practices. Setting Prices Cost-plus pricing sets the price of an item by adding a percen
Offers prices that are in line with how much buyers value a product.

A share-driven pricing
approach may be effective if
- The revenue increase from more first-time purchases compensates for the smaller profit margins. - Customers have reason to return and purchase again. - The lower prices do not weaken the brand.

The Relationship Between Value and Price
Use value or utility
A product’s functional benefits plus the amount of pleasure or satisfaction that it gives to a buyer.
The amount of money that people are willing to pay for a product or service. exchange reference differentiavalue = value + tion value
The price at which a product or service is offered by most sellers.
The price of the product or service that is the next best alternative—the competing product that is closest to a given product in use value—to a given product.
The difference in use value (monetary or psychological) between a product and its next best alternative.
The monetary gain or savings a product offers relative to the next best alternative. Differentiation Value $ Monetary Value Psychological Value

The amount of pleasure or satisfaction a product provides compared to the next best alternative. Measuring Psychological Value It’s possible to measure a product’s psychological differentiation value indirectly–through testing, surveys or A/B tests, how much customers will pay for it compared to the next best alternative.
If you were buying your best friend a cake, which option would you choose? A B Size 12” 12” Price $3 $4
Guidelines used to keep pricing decisions aligned with the marketing strategy, with the goal of maximizing long-term profit.

A pricing strategy in which a firm initially sets a high price to capture consumer surplus from early adopters willing to pay more, then gradually lowers the price over time to attract more price-sensitive segments of the market.
Building a large customer base by starting with a low price.
A relationship between two interdependent products whereby you can only extract value from either product by purchasing both (e.g., a printer and its proprietary ink cartridge).
An increase in a product’s value as more people use it.
A strategy for retaining market share despite competitor price cuts by increasing value to customers and keeping the price as is.

Determining the Right Price Level
Price window

The range between the lowest price and the highest price of a product or service. In a larger price window, the upper boundary = total use value, and the lower = marginal cost. A narrower price window uses the price of the next best alternative as its upper boundary and the price of the inferior alternative as its lower boundary. $4,000 Upper bound

Examples of Different
Classes of Goods

CONSUMER SEGMENTATION BY PRICE SENSITIVITY

Price Segmentation

Benefits and Challenges Price segmentation–offering different prices to different customer segments that vary in price sensitivity–can increase total revenue. Cannibalization occurs when customers who are willing to pay more for a product end up paying less after we introduce a lower-priced offer. Adding a lower-priced offer can still be a winning

Consumers who belong to a specified group qualify for a discount while others do not. In order for group pricing to be effective the following requirements must be met: Membership in the defined group is easy to confirm. Membership in the defined group correlates with a certain level of price sensitivity (e.g., full-time students have less money to

Group Pricing 30% off with a student ID Regional Pricing Barilla pasta is cheaper in Italy than in Sweden. Channel Pricing Bottled water is more expensive at airports. Time-based Tacos are half-price Pricing during happy hour. Volume Discounts Bagels are priced at $1 each and $5 for a dozen. Couponing Adidas shoes are 20% off with a discount code.
Charging different prices for the same product across different distribution channels.
Charging different prices based on the customer’s time of purchase.
Charging a lower price per unit when a product is purchased in higher quantities.
Offering discount certificates or discount codes.
A product version with greater perceived utility than the base model, offered as a more expensive option for customers willing to pay more.
A product version with less perceived utility than the base model, offered as a less expensive option for customers who are only willing to pay less.
A product that’s been re-engineered to have less utility than the base model, so it can be offered at a lower price without cannibalizing revenue from those willing to pay more.
Consumers’ tendency to avoid choosing the most expensive product (e.g., for fear of overpaying) and the least expensive product (e.g., for fear of buying a lowquality product).

The approach of offering three versions of a product, so that there is a mid-priced option that is generally perceived as a safe choice for consumers. Bundling To benefit from extreme aversion, position the version you aim to sell the most units of as the “better” option: Price Good Better Best $200 $300 $700 It’s best to display the savings on eac

Offering a set of products at a price that is less than the sum of the products’ individual prices. Pure bundling offers customers only the bundle option and not the option of buying the components in the set separately. Savings $500 $250 $200 Coffee Table $200 $150 $50 Total $2,500 $1,500 $1,000

Rules for designing and
executing price promotions

1. Target consumers who wouldn’t otherwise buy your product. 2. Make the promotional periods brief. 3. Make the discounts inconsistent and irregular. 4. Present the discount as a unique, one-time event that isn’t indicative of future prices.

A permanent price reduction in response to declining demand. To maximize revenue from a markdown scheme, both the starting price and the price you mark down to matter. For example: The revenue for Scheme B ($24,000 + $18,000 – $6,000 =$36,000) is greater than Scheme A ($24,000 + $25,000 – $15,000 = $34,000): $24,000 Scheme A $24,000 $80 $80 $50 $50

Situations in which a markdown scheme can maximize revenue. For a markdown scheme to succeed in maximizing revenue, those willing to pay full price must buy at that price instead of waiting for the markdown. They have incentive to do so if: • They think the product is limited in supply. • The product declines in perceived utility over time due to t

1. Supply is limited and fixed. 2. The value of the item plummets if it isn’t sold by a certain perishable date. 3. The quantity of demand holds constant or declines as the perishable date approaches.

management

Competitive Pricing in E-Commerce
Price transparency
the ease of accessing the range of prices offered—is heightened in e-commerce. This often drives prices down.
Web search tools that gather and present prices from multiple e-commerce sites.
the range of prices paid for the same item—is high in e-commerce despite increased price transparency.
the ease of accessing information about a product—varies greatly in e-commerce. Better product transparency (greater ease of search, sharper images, etc.) can be an effective price fence.
To maintain high prices in e-commerce
• Provide good order fulfillment and delivery services

costs

Risks range from low priority (unlikely to occur and unlikely to do much harm) to high priority (likely to occur and would do significant harm). Risk management is the practice of identifying, prioritizing, and responding to risks as part of project management. Risks can be

• Worker-paced assembly line flow: workers control the speed of their individual tasks, and the overall process progresses based on their pace (example: computer manufacturer)

Job shops often group machines by type to accommodate a variety of products. Flow shops usually arrange machines sequentially to match the steps of the production process (think assembly line). • Machine-paced assembly line flow: the production rate is dictated by machinery rather than by human workers (example: auto manufacturer)

These categories aren’t mutually exclusive. • Batch processing: products and services are delivered in groups or batches rather than continuously (example: buffet restaurant) • Continuous flow: designed for the production of highly standardized products that are in constant, very high demand (example: oil refinery)

• Pipeline: anything still moving through production, supply, or service processes but not yet finished or ready • Buffer: inventory between activities that helps them function more independently of each other • Seasonal: inventory accumulated during periods of low demand to prepare for predictable surges during peak seasons • Safety: inventory kep
Use COGS as a single unifying measure when dealing with disparate types and values of inventory (e.g., a retailer that sells televisions and toothpicks).

Optimizing Labor Resources
Metrics for employee productivity include

• Idle time: periods when a resource, such as labor or machinery, isn't actively being used for production • Utilization: how much of a resource's capacity is actively being used compared to its full potential.

chapters.title: Supply Chain & Operations
chapters.intro: Operations management transforms inputs into outputs efficiently and reliably. This chapter covers project management (scope, schedule, budget, risk), process design and flow analysis, capacity planning and resource allocation, managing variability and uncertainty, demand forecasting methods, and supply chain coordination from supplier to end customer.

rows: These formulas only apply when utilization is less than one. When utilization is greater than or equal to one, the queue is unstable.
when customer demand for a product cannot be met because the item is out of stock. Stockouts can lead to lost sales, reduced customer satisfaction, and potential longterm damage to brand loyalty.

a metric used to evaluate forecast accuracy, calculated by dividing actual demand by the forecasted demand for a given period. • An A/F ratio of 1 means the forecast was accurate. • A ratio below 1 indicates overforecasting (excessive optimism). • A ratio above 1 indicates underforecasting (excessive pessimism).

an estimate of the typical relationship between actual demand and forecasted demand, based on historical data. It’s used to adjust future forecasts to account for consistent over- or underestimation. If the expected A/F ratio is 0.85, for example, future forecasts might be multiplied by 0.85 to better reflect likely actual demand. The Newsvendor Mo

THE NEWSVENDOR MODEL

Expected profit-maximizing order quantity

the inventory level that balances the cost of overstocking and the cost of stockouts to achieve the highest possible expected profit. It’s the optimal number of units a business should produce or order when demand is uncertain, based on probabilities and cost-benefit tradeoffs.

the amount a business can recover from an unsold product, typically by selling it as parts, scrap, or at a discount. It helps offset the cost of overproduction and is used when calculating overage cost.

the loss incurred for each unit of inventory that goes unsold. It is calculated as the difference between the cost to produce (or acquire) the unit and its salvage value. Underage cost represents the opportunity cost of missing a sale due to not having enough inventory. It’s calculated as the retail price minus the cost to produce or acquire—in oth

• Adjust the demand forecast for forecasting error to get the expected actual demand. • Determine the opportunity cost of being one unit short (underage cost) versus the cost of having one unit too many (overage cost). • Calculate the critical ratio to find the probability at which the expected underage cost equals expected overage cost. • Identify
the number of units that can’t be sold due to insufficient inventory to meet actual demand.

the mathematical function used to calculate the expected number of lost unit sales based on the standard normal distribution. It helps determine the shortfall in sales when inventory is insufficient to meet actual demand.
the number of units remaining after actual demand has been met. 190
Identify the desired probability of meeting customer demand without stockouts. •
Calculate the quantity of inventory required to meet the chosen service level. •
Order enough stock to bring the inventory back to the target level, avoiding both under-ordering and over-ordering.
the annual cost of carrying one unit in inventory, expressed as a percentage of the unit’s cost and determined by the company’s accountants.
the likelihood that the seller will have the product available when a customer wants to buy it, based on the quantity kept in stock and demand from other customers.

THE PROBLEM WITH RECURRING DEMAND

Inventory position

a measure of the total inventory available to meet demand, calculated as the sum of on-hand inventory and on-order inventory, minus any back orders. In a pull system, inventory is ordered in response to actual demand. In a push system, inventory is ordered in anticipation of expected demand. 192

Incentive Conflicts in Supply Chains
The problem

• Each player in a supply chain may have different prices and costs, so the risk of carrying too much or too little supply can vary between players. • Such incentive conflicts often lead to significant mismatches in demand across the supply chain.
The fraction of the optimal profit a supply chain achieves. • As a result, the supply chain doesn’t reach maximal efficiency and individual firms don’t maximize their profits.

• Increase the expected profit-maximizing order quantity of each player in the supply chain. • To incentivize each player to order more, increase its critical ratio—i.e., reduce its overage cost or increase its underage cost, or both. • A seller can increase a buyer’s critical ratio by offering a buyback contract, where the seller agrees to buy bac
Making the Deal...............................206 Cooperative Strategy and Strategic Alliances................................ 210

Alternatives to Vertical Integration
Long-term contract
Lasts more than one year and concerns multiple, or repeated, transactions.

One firm (the franchisor) grants use of its assets to another (the franchisee) for commercial use. The franchisee shoulders much of the risk in this arrangement. A long-term contract brings more reliability to the supply chain, but also means a firm is “locked-in.”
Two or more firms partner to make or sell offerings together. The firms remain wholly separate but are still able to coordinate as if one. Still, a setback for one could hurt all.

THE DECISION TO DIVERSIFY

Types and Modes of Diversification
Diversification

When a company expands into a market that’s different from its core business. • Horizontal diversification: A company sells products that are unrelated to their existing offerings but appeal to the same audience. • Concentric diversification: A company offers related products to an entirely new customer base. • Conglomerate diversification: A compa

• Improving returns Vertical Integration vs. Diversification Vertical integration occurs when a firm takes over more of a product’s value chain. Diversification occurs when a firm expands into an entirely new value chain. • Mitigating risk

When diversifying for strategic reasons, it’s important to be conscious of antitrust and competition laws. • Bargaining power: Pressure from suppliers or buyers that influences the cost of conducting exchange in a market. • Price war: When sellers compete for a larger market share by slashing prices.

An acquisition may also be referred to as a takeover.
Merger

The belief that the sum of a firm’s parts performs better than they would individually. The two types of synergy are: 1. Operating synergy: Operational efficiency is improved by combining multiple parts. 2. Financial synergy: Cost of capital is lowered by combining multiple parts. M&A activity may help with strategic realignment, when a company rap

M&A activity may be used to increase a firm’s assets or diversify its offerings. Historical Trends in M&A Performance Periods of significant M&A activity tend to be followed by periods of less activity; these are known as merger waves.
Lessons from merger waves include the following

• Periods of high economic growth tend to increase M&A activity, while recessions tend to reduce M&A activity. • Waves can consist of a series of conglomerate creations, friendly or unfriendly acquisitions, and more. Merger waves may be caused by shocks in a firm’s operating environment, such as: • deregulation • technological innovation • and more

Returns not due to systematic or marketwide influences. Abnormal returns are used to assess shareholder returns during and after M&A activity. Target firm shareholders tend to enjoy abnormal returns during the premerger phase, while bidding firm shareholders do not. Cash deals, private target firms, and relatively small bidding firms tend to see in

An investment strategy that seeks to capture the difference between a share’s market price and the price offered in a proposal. Hedge funds often play the role of arbitrageur, a person or organization that profits by exploiting a market inefficiency. Hedge funds: Portfolios focused on highly-liquid assets.
More regulated portfolios open to the general public.
Portfolios that focus on large, longer-term investments in companies.

Laws that prevent any one firm from controlling a market. Takeovers An acquirer is the parent company; a target is the subsidiary company. A subsidiary can be either partly or wholly owned. Acquiring a wholly owned subsidiary requires buying 100% of its stock.
Two-tier tender offer: Offering a higher price for a set percentage of shares.
Accusing the board of not upholding their fiduciary duty. 203

The Cost of Acquisition
Acquisition premium
The price of an offer over the target’s current share price.
A target shareholder’s gains may be increased if he waits for other shareholders to sell at a lower price.
When cash flows from a target company are used to pay down debt from the takeover.

Amendments to a company’s charter or bylaws. * Fair price provisions: Requiring bidders to pay all shareholders the same premium. * Golden parachute: Requiring the acquirer to pay expensive severance packages to management.

Target firm shareholders can buy discounted shares in the bidding company, post acquisition. Shark repellents increase board influence, slow board turnover, or limit shareholder power. Shark repellents include super majority rules, advanced notice provisions, super voting stock, and “for cause” provisions. Poison pills are triggered by an investor

Takeover defenses used after a hostile takeover has begun. * Debt-based defense: Taking on large debts post offer. * Greenmail: Paying a hostile bidder a premium for shares. * A company may restructure its assets or legal structure. * White knight: A preferred buyer to the current bidder. Debt-based defense is also known as leveraged recapitalizati

THE PRACTICE OF M&A

The Acquisition Plan and Target Selection
Acquisition plan
A comprehensive document addressing the strategic goals, selection criteria, and timetable for a takeover.
Stipulates that the contents of a negotiation will be kept secret, describes the information that can be shared amongst the parties, and more.
There are two types of sales
• Asset sale: The buyer receives the target’s assets. • Stock sale: The purchasing company buys ownership shares in the target.

Closing the Deal
Definitive purchase agreement
A document that finalizes terms and establishes the conditions for a deal’s completion.

A major, negative change in the target firm’s circumstances. Such a change may allow the buyer to back out of the deal. The flow of funds provides a financial breakdown of the deal. The flow of funds contains information like account numbers, who is paying, and which stakeholders are receiving payment. Post-Merger Integration A management integrati

The MIT will create an integration plan, which typically
includes four steps
1. Gather key information. 2. Determine key milestones and their completion time. 3. Communicate the plan to stakeholders. 4. Execute the integration plan.

It’s always a good idea to do a cultural assessment of the two companies. Financial executives, human resource experts, and IT people should be members of the MIT. Functional integration involves verifying due diligence, setting benchmarks, developing a plan to meet benchmarks, and monitoring. 207
The buyer pledges assets as collateral for a loan. This is also known as secured lending.

For

There are four general buying behaviors

People react differently to choices based on how they're presented. The endowment effect suggests people endow an object with more value simply because they own it, and not because of any intrinsic value the object may have.

• Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), which detects active changes in blood flow and oxygenation in the brain. • Electroencephalography (EEG), which records electrical activity in the brain.

exceptionally high levels of satisfaction and positive emotions.

A measurement of how much demand changes relative to a given change in price. A product with demand that changes more as a result of a changed price is more elastic than one that changes little with a change in price.
