DeFi Protocols
Five protocols are the basic building blocks of DeFi

1. Decentralized stablecoins are tokens that are pegged to a fiat currency without requiring a centralized custodian to manage a reserve. 2. Decentralized lending involves individual liquidity providers (LPs) making assets available for others to borrow.

Supply and demand are controlled by a combination of market forces and human or algorithmic interventions, all of which are encoded in a smart contract. Loans are typically overcollateralized because of the volatility of the tokens or cryptocurrency that serves as the collateral. LPs earn interest and lending protocols earn fees. 3. Decentralized e
• Bring off-chain assets onchain • Create fractional ownership of an otherwise indivisible asset

rows: With flash loans, if any part of the combined transaction fails, the entire transaction is canceled. This eliminates counterparty risk and thus the need for collateral.
Uncollateralized stablecoins free capital that would otherwise be locked in a reserve to be used in productive ways.
By reducing the costs associated with contracts, blockchains can facilitate contracts with small amounts of value.
Blockchains can improve transparency around data collection and make data less susceptible to alteration. Eliminating a central server may also reduce costs.
By interfacing with oracles that provide trusted information, blockchains can host apps that offer cryptographically secure identity data that can be unlocked by an individual’s digital signature.

Blockchain Disadvantages
Some disadvantages of blockchain include

• Contracts are more difficult to understand because they’re written in computer code. • Oracles may receive false external information. • Invalid transactions on public blockchains cannot generally be rolled back. • The value of cryptocurrency can be volatile. • The regulatory and legal implications of blockchain are still uncertain. • The demand

The availability of a finalizing chain that no centralized authority can shut down. Ethereum can handle a maximum of 116.7 transactions per second, compared to Visa, which handles 65,000+ transactions per second.

BEYOND THE BASICS OF BLOCKCHAIN

Smart Contracts
Contract
An agreement to exchange things or actions of value.
Computer code that implements a contract.
When triggered and conditions are fulfilled, the program runs and executes the contract without human intervention.

An Application on the Blockchain
Application (app)
Computer code that allows a user to interact with data.
A type of application that accesses at least part of its functionality and data through the internet.

A multi-user app that uses smart contracts on a blockchain to securely store and process its data. The conditions and instructions of the Dapp’s code are in the smart contract. All other information is stored in its data storage.
Provides a shared service that’s controlled by a single entity. An example of a smart contract if spear_owner is spike and wheel_owner is grog: set spear_owner to grog set wheel_owner to spike
Dapps have three advantages over centralized apps

1. Censorship resistance: Freedom from third-party interference. 2. Resiliency: The ability to carry on in the face of outages. 3. Transparency: Confidence in the integrity of the Dapp’s data and logic.
The “critical data” in a Dapp is data that needs the protection of a blockchain: censorship resistance, resiliency, and transparency.

Dapp Architecture
Dapps are typically divided into two parts

1. A front end, which interacts with the user on his or her device. 2. A back end, which does the bulk of the data processing. In a Dapp, the back end secures the critical data. Decentralized Dapp Front Ends A decentralized storage system stores data across a distributed peer-to-peer network in a way that allows for censorship resistance, resilienc
The unique string of numbers and letters that identifies an Ethereum account.

Used to describe a token’s economics. Developers can monetize their Dapp by charging fees for specific actions or selling unique digital assets. This includes anything that influences a token’s value, such as the timing of its creation, its supply and demand, and the rate at which it is burned.

The colors of slide backgrounds, headers, and text. Coordinating colors, and using fewer colors, gives a deck unity and clarity.

During a presentation, be sure to

Delivering Your Presentation
Personalization principle
the idea that people learn better when information is delivered in a conversational style, rather than a formal, impersonal tone.
the deliberate choice of language that builds confidence, reduces perceived risk, and positions the recommended strategy as the logical path forward.
the specific information about performance that helps refine skills, correct mistakes, and reinforce strengths.

• Simulating the presentation prepares for presentation constraints. • Using a mirror or recording yourself makes aware your pacing, tone, and body language. • Using a timer helps presentation meet the allotted time.

To get helpful feedback, ask targeted questions that focus on specific aspects of your presentation, such as, "Did a feeling of urgency come across in my conclusion?" • Rehearing in front of others gets immediate, objective feedback.
• Record practice sessions • Use thoughtful phrasing to keep audience engaged • Within reason,use multiple takes and editing to enhance the presentation

Any outside factors that can influence the success of the business. In the business plan, it’s accounted for in the business environment analysis.

A sole trader is attractive because of its simplicity: • The owner doesn’t need to register with Companies House, so information is private. • The set up costs are very low. • The owner only needs to register with Revenues and Customs when making over £1000 in a fiscal year. • The owner pays personal income taxes on profits.

There are two types of partners

Remember: Partners in a GP can be individuals or limited liability businesses. Salaried partners take a base salary rather than a share of the profits and don’t usually have ownership in the business.

Limited Liability Partnership
Limited liability partnership (LLP)
A business owned collectively by a member of a group, wherein the business is a distinct legal body separate from those members.
Members of the LLP are not exposed to the business’s debts.
Once an LLP is established, its members

• Can enter into contracts on behalf of the LLP. • Can inspect all LLP records. • Can’t be expelled without consent from other members. Similarities with General Partnerships • LLPs should have a partnership agreement. • Owners tend to be involved in day-to-day management. • Other businesses can have ownership of an LLP. • Start up costs are low, a

A business structure that has a separate legal identity from its owners and is run by directors. • Public companies can be listed on the stock exchange and sell shares to the general public. • Private companies can’t be listed on the stock exchange or sell shares to the general public, and they tend to have more involved owners.

EMPLOYEES AND THEIR RIGHTS

Employment Status
Employment status

A classification that determines working people’s rights and an employer’s responsibilities. An employee’s primary function is to meet the needs of their employer by following strict guidelines. The employer, for their part, must provide work to the employee. A self-employed independent contractor provides services to a client, usually under a writ
Rights and protections differ depending on status

right/protection discrimination protection health & safety protection minimum wage paid holidays rest breaks pension enrollment unfair dismissal protection minimum dismissal notice statutory redundancy pay sick pay paid parental leave employee contractor    right/protection         worker   X X X • Employers control what employees work on and how a
Employers can prioritize applicants with protected characteristics who are underrepresented or socially disadvantaged.

When a seemingly neutral policy has a harmful effect on someone with a protected characteristic. The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) enforces and promotes non-discrimination laws. Protected characteristics age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion and belief, sex, and s
An agreement that outlines the conditions of employment and the employee’s rights and responsibilities.
Stipulations that clarify the agreement between the employer and employee. There are two types:

Employment contracts apply to workers and employees—but not contractors, who draw up their own terms and conditions. • Express terms: Terms that must be specifically outlined and agreed to (e.g., name of employer). • Implied terms: Terms that may be too obvious to be written out (e.g., like following the law).

Separate stipulations in an employment contract. • Restrictive covenant clause: Imposes certain posttermination restrictions to protect a business’s information or customer and employee identities. • Confidentiality clause: Specifies how a recipient of important or private information can share or use that information.

Basic Labor Standards
Basic labor standards
Establish a baseline for things like wages, equal pay, and working time, and all employers must follow them.
The same compensation for jobs that have similar tasks, similar demands, or create equivalent value, regardless of gender.
A non-gender related rationale for a difference in pay.

Any time an employee is at the disposal of their employer. Workers between school leaving age and the age of 25 are entitled to the national minimum wage, while workers older than 25 qualify for a slightly higher national living wage. The maximum number of working hours a week is an average of 48 hours over 17 weeks.

Employee Protections and Benefits
Health and Safety Executive (HSE)

The government agency that enforces workplace health and safety. Serious workplace injuries, diseases, or near-misses must be reported under Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations (RIDDOR). Don’t forget: RIDDOR applies to members of the public injured around a workplace!

A reduced wage for sick employees covering up to 28 weeks per year. Statutory paternity and maternity leave provides 2 weeks off for fathers and 52 weeks off for mothers at a reduced wage. Holiday entitlement provides 28 days of paid vacation. Employers must provide a private pension for all employees over the age 22 making at least £10,000 a year.
The end of an employment contract for any reason.

When an employee’s contract is ended by the employer. • Unfair dismissal: When an employer doesn’t have cause for dismissal, violates the law, or fails to follow the company’s formal dismissal process. • Constructive dismissal: When an employee is forced to quit their job because of severe misconduct by the employer. Reasons for Fair Dismissal • Ab
An employer and employee come to an agreement to terminate the employment contract together.
An employee simply resigns from their job.
An agreement that grants another party permission to use an asset without risk of infringement.
A creator allows another party to duplicate their business and assets in exchange for royalties.
Provides government-granted ownership over an invention for up to 20 years. To qualify, an invention must be: • New • Inventive (or beyond a simple modification) • Something that can be made or used
Indefinite protection for any sign that represents a business or brand.
When someone uses an identical or confusingly similar mark without permission.
Trademark protection provided in the event that an unregistered trademark is used to mislead consumers.
A free, automatic right that protects creative expressions that are: • Written or recorded • The author’s original work What might a trademark protect?
An international agreement protecting artistic works under a common framework.
Works where the public has the rights of the original rights-holder to reproduce the work as they wish.

Exceptions to copyright law that allow for limited use of copyright without getting permission from the owner. A design refers to an object’s appearance, shape, configuration, and/or decoration. A registered design must: • Be new • Be inoffensive • Not use protected emblems (e.g., flags) • Not be an invention

A design right ensuring that no one can intentionally or unintentionally reproduce a registered design. Entrepreneurial Networks and Networking
Entrepreneurial network
A system for sharing resources that's arranged to serve the unique needs of business ventures.
The individual actors in a network.
The relationships between nodes in a network.

When nodes are so similar to, focused on, or biased in favor of each other that the whole network becomes insulated. Small Worlds 301
Asking a specific node about a group they're tied to.
Asking a wide group of nodes about a particular resource.
Searching for short paths by asking only nodes in a network who seem likely to have the most ties to the most diverse information.

Structural Holes and Brokering
Structural holes
Gaps in a network, or the absence of direct ties between its nodes.
Creating ties that span structural holes.

Nodes that bridge structural holes. Insiders and Outsiders An insider is part of an exclusive network. Steps in Bridging 1. Perceive an unmet need and underemployed resources. 2. Discover fits between an unmet need and underemployed resources. 3. Create a product or service that embodies the most valuable fit.

An outsider lacks high-profile, exclusive connections.
Preferential attachment
The systemic process by which nodes are favored for additional resources because of how much they already have.
A lack of status or influence with the gatekeepers that mediate resource distribution.
Insiders who advocate for an outsider and actively use their influence to obtain resources.

Supply chains support three main entities in the production
cycle

Three phases of supply chains identify needs create systems maintain systems 1. planning 2. making 3. enabling • The supplier who provides raw materials. • The manufacturer who transforms raw materials into goods. • The buyer who consumes the goods.

A supply chain comprises three phases that inform one
another

Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)
The activities that go into getting working software into the hands of users.

The team establishes a vision and scope for the project and assesses the feasibility and business case. The phases of the SDLC aren't necessarily sequential— activities from multiple phases can take place at the same time.
The capabilities a system must have and the conditions it must meet.

One programmer writes code and the other reviews it as it's being written.

The team checks to see if the code works as intended. Testing should be automated as much as possible. • Unit testing: Testing individual units of code in isolation. • Integration testing: Testing how related components integrate and work together. • System testing: Testing how the overall system works.
The team publishes the software (or rolls out changes to the software) and the system goes live.

A set of computers on which the system is run and tested. • Development environment: Offers a space separate from users and system testers for writing code. • Staging environment: Allows for testing of the overall system with mock datasets. • Production environment: Provides a live, stable version for users to access.

reference.tax_reference_markdown:
# Verified Tax Numbers for 2025 and 2026

## Source: IRS Notice 2025-67 (IR-2025-111, Nov 13, 2025)

### Retirement Plan Contribution Limits

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| 401(k)/403(b)/457(b)/TSP deferral limit | $23,500 | $24,500 |
| 401(k) catch-up (age 50-59, 64+) | $7,500 | $8,000 |
| 401(k) super catch-up (age 60-63, SECURE 2.0) | $11,250 | $11,250 |
| Total 401(k) with catch-up (50-59, 64+) | $31,000 | $32,500 |
| Total 401(k) with super catch-up (60-63) | $34,750 | $35,750 |
| IRA contribution limit | $7,000 | $7,500 |
| IRA catch-up (age 50+) | $1,000 | $1,100 |
| SIMPLE IRA deferral | $16,500 | $17,000 |
| SIMPLE IRA (certain applicable plans) | $17,600 | $18,100 |
| SIMPLE catch-up (age 50+) | $3,500 | $4,000 |
| SIMPLE catch-up (certain applicable, 50+) | $3,850 | $3,850 |
| SIMPLE super catch-up (age 60-63) | $5,250 | $5,250 |
| Defined contribution limit (415(c)) | $70,000 | $72,000 |
| SEP contribution limit | $70,000 | $72,000 |
| Annual compensation limit (401(a)(17)) | $350,000 | $360,000 |
| Roth catch-up wage threshold (SECURE 2.0) | $145,000 | $150,000 |
| Highly compensated employee threshold | $160,000 | $160,000 |

**SECURE 2.0 Note (effective 2026):** Participants with prior-year FICA wages exceeding $150,000 must make catch-up contributions as designated Roth contributions.

### IRA Phase-Out Ranges (Traditional IRA Deduction)

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single (covered by workplace plan) | $79,000-$89,000 | $81,000-$91,000 |
| MFJ (contributing spouse covered) | $126,000-$146,000 | $129,000-$149,000 |
| MFJ (contributor NOT covered, spouse IS) | $236,000-$246,000 | $242,000-$252,000 |
| MFS (covered by workplace plan) | $0-$10,000 | $0-$10,000 |

### Roth IRA Income Phase-Out Ranges

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single/HOH | $150,000-$165,000 | $153,000-$168,000 |
| MFJ | $236,000-$246,000 | $242,000-$252,000 |
| MFS | $0-$10,000 | $0-$10,000 |

### Saver's Credit Income Limits

| Filing Status | 2025 | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| MFJ | $79,000 | $80,500 |
| HOH | $59,250 | $60,375 |
| Single/MFS | $39,500 | $40,250 |

### HSA Contribution Limits

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| HSA self-only | $4,300 | $4,400 |
| HSA family | $8,550 | $8,750 |
| HSA catch-up (age 55+) | $1,000 | $1,000 |
| HDHP minimum deductible (self-only) | $1,650 | $1,700 |
| HDHP minimum deductible (family) | $3,300 | $3,400 |
| HDHP max out-of-pocket (self-only) | $8,300 | $8,500 |
| HDHP max out-of-pocket (family) | $16,600 | $17,000 |

### Gift and Estate Tax

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| Annual gift tax exclusion | $19,000 | $19,000 |
| Gift to non-citizen spouse | $190,000 | $194,000 |
| Estate/gift tax unified credit (OBBBA) | $13,990,000 | $15,000,000 |
| Top estate/gift/GST tax rate | 40% | 40% |

Note: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA, signed July 4, 2025) made the TCJA estate tax exemption PERMANENT and raised it to $15 million per person ($30 million per married couple with portability) beginning in 2026, indexed for inflation from 2027 onward. Step-up in basis at death is preserved. No changes to marginal estate tax rates, valuation rules, or trust taxation.

### Standard Deduction (Source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32, with OBBBA amendments)

| Filing Status | 2025 (post-OBBBA) | 2026 |
|--------------|------|------|
| Single/MFS | $15,750 | $16,100 |
| MFJ | $31,500 | $32,200 |
| HOH | $23,625 | $24,150 |
| Additional (65+/blind, single/HOH) | $2,000 | $2,050 |
| Additional (65+/blind, MFJ per person) | $1,600 | $1,650 |

**OBBBA Note:** The standard deduction was permanently extended and increased above pre-OBBBA levels. OBBBA retroactively raised 2025 amounts (from $15,000 single / $30,000 MFJ to $15,750 / $31,500). Personal exemptions remain permanently at $0.

### OBBBA Senior Deduction (TEMPORARY: 2025-2028 only)

| Item | Amount |
|------|--------|
| Deduction per qualifying taxpayer (age 65+) | $6,000 |
| MFJ (both spouses 65+) | $12,000 |
| Phase-out threshold (Single/HOH) | $75,000 MAGI |
| Phase-out threshold (MFJ) | $150,000 MAGI |
| Phase-out rate | 6% of MAGI exceeding threshold |
| Full phase-out (Single) | $175,000 |
| Full phase-out (MFJ) | $350,000 |
| Availability | Both itemizers and standard deduction filers |
| Ineligible status | Married Filing Separately |

**Critical:** This deduction is TEMPORARY (2025-2028) and is separate from both the standard deduction and the existing additional standard deduction for age 65+. It does not require receipt of Social Security benefits.

### SALT Deduction (OBBBA — new for 2025)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| SALT deduction cap | $40,000 | $40,400 |
| Phase-down threshold (MAGI) | $500,000 | $500,500 |
| Phase-down rate | 30% of MAGI excess | 30% of MAGI excess |
| SALT floor (cannot reduce below) | $10,000 | $10,000 |
| Expiration | End of 2029 | — |

**Note:** OBBBA raised the SALT cap from $10,000 (TCJA) to $40,000 for 2025, indexed thereafter. High-income taxpayers face a phase-down that can reduce the cap to $10,000.

### Social Security (Source: SSA COLA Fact Sheet 2026)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| COLA increase | 2.5% | 2.8% |
| SS wage base (OASDI) | $176,100 | $184,500 |
| Medicare wage base | No limit | No limit |
| Additional Medicare tax (>$200K single / >$250K MFJ) | 0.9% | 0.9% |
| Earnings test (under FRA) | $23,400/yr ($1,950/mo) | $24,480/yr ($2,040/mo) |
| Earnings test (year of FRA) | $62,160/yr ($5,180/mo) | $65,160/yr ($5,430/mo) |
| Max SS benefit at FRA | $4,018/mo | $4,152/mo |
| Quarter of coverage | $1,810 | $1,890 |
| SGA (non-blind) | $1,620/mo | $1,690/mo |
| SGA (blind) | $2,700/mo | $2,830/mo |

### 2026 Federal Income Tax Brackets (Source: IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32)

| Rate | Single | MFJ | HOH |
|------|--------|-----|-----|
| 10% | $0-$12,400 | $0-$24,800 | $0-$17,700 |
| 12% | $12,401-$50,400 | $24,801-$100,800 | $17,701-$67,450 |
| 22% | $50,401-$105,700 | $100,801-$211,400 | $67,451-$105,700 |
| 24% | $105,701-$201,775 | $211,401-$403,550 | $105,701-$201,775 |
| 32% | $201,776-$256,225 | $403,551-$512,450 | $201,776-$256,200 |
| 35% | $256,226-$640,600 | $512,451-$768,700 | $256,201-$640,600 |
| 37% | $640,601+ | $768,701+ | $640,601+ |

**OBBBA Note:** The 37% top rate is now permanent. OBBBA provided an additional ~4% inflation adjustment for the bottom two brackets (10% and 12%) versus ~2.3% for higher brackets, creating slightly more favorable treatment for lower-income ranges.

### 2026 Capital Gains Tax Brackets

| Rate | Single | MFJ | HOH |
|------|--------|-----|-----|
| 0% | $0-$49,450 | $0-$98,900 | $0-$66,200 |
| 15% | $49,451-$545,500 | $98,901-$613,700 | $66,201-$579,600 |
| 20% | $545,501+ | $613,701+ | $579,601+ |

Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) of 3.8% applies on lesser of net investment income or MAGI exceeding $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ). These thresholds are NOT inflation-indexed.

### AMT Exemptions 2026

| Filing Status | Exemption | Phase-out Begins | 28% Rate Applies Above |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Single | $90,100 | $500,000 | $244,500 |
| MFJ | $140,200 | $1,000,000 | $244,500 |

**OBBBA Note:** AMT exemptions made permanent. Phase-out thresholds were reduced from 2025 levels ($625,350 single / $1,252,700 MFJ) back to approximately 2018 levels, and the phase-out rate increased from 25% to 50%. This represents a slight tax increase for some high-income filers.

### QBI Deduction (Section 199A) — 2026

| Filing Status | Phase-out Begins | Phase-out Ends |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------|
| Single | $201,775 | $276,775 |
| MFJ | $403,500 | $553,500 |

**OBBBA Note:** QBI deduction made permanent. Phase-out range expanded from $50,000 to $75,000 (single) and from $100,000 to $150,000 (MFJ). New $400 minimum deduction added for taxpayers below phase-out threshold.

### Medicare 2026 (Source: CMS Fact Sheet, Nov 14, 2025)

| Item | 2025 | 2026 |
|------|------|------|
| Part B standard monthly premium | $185.00 | $202.90 |
| Part B annual deductible | $257 | $283 |
| Part A inpatient deductible | $1,676 | $1,736 |
| Part A daily coinsurance (days 61-90) | $419 | $434 |
| Part A lifetime reserve day coinsurance | $838 | $868 |
| SNF daily coinsurance (days 21-100) | $209.50 | $217.00 |

### Medicare IRMAA Brackets 2026 (Single Filers)

| Income (MAGI) | Part B Premium | Part D Surcharge |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|
| <= $109,000 | $202.90 | $0.00 |
| $109,001-$137,000 | $284.10 | $14.50 |
| $137,001-$171,000 | $405.80 | $37.50 |
| $171,001-$205,000 | $527.50 | $60.40 |
| $205,001-$499,999 | $649.20 | $83.30 |
| >= $500,000 | $689.90 | $91.00 |

For MFJ, double the income thresholds.

### EITC 2026

| Children | Max Credit | Phaseout Ends (Single) | Phaseout Ends (MFJ) |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 0 | $664 | $19,540 | $26,820 |
| 1 | $4,427 | $51,593 | $58,863 |
| 2 | $7,316 | $58,629 | $65,899 |
| 3+ | $8,231 | $62,974 | $70,224 |

### Child Tax Credit 2026
- Maximum CTC: $2,200 per qualifying child (OBBBA made permanent, inflation-adjusted)
- Refundable portion: $1,700
- Phase-out begins: $200,000 (single) / $400,000 (MFJ)

### Key Legislative Changes (OBBBA — One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025)

**Permanent provisions:**
- Made TCJA individual tax provisions PERMANENT (37% top rate, expanded brackets, higher standard deduction)
- Estate tax exemption set at $15M per person (permanent, inflation-adjusted from 2027)
- QBI deduction made permanent with expanded phase-out range and $400 minimum
- CTC increased to $2,200 (permanent, inflation-adjusted)
- Personal exemption remains $0 (permanent)
- AMT higher exemptions made permanent (but phase-out thresholds reduced)
- Standard deduction boost made permanent (retroactive to 2025)
- Itemized deduction limitation (Pease) remains eliminated

**Temporary provisions (2025-2028):**
- $6,000 senior deduction for age 65+ (phases out at $75K/$150K MAGI)
- Tax-free tip income deduction (up to $25,000)
- Tax-free overtime income deduction

**Temporary provisions (2025-2029):**
- SALT deduction cap increased to $40,000 (from $10,000), with phase-down for high earners

**Other notable changes:**
- QSBS (Section 1202): Holding period shortened from 5 to 3 years, exclusion increased from $10M to $15M, gross asset limit raised from $50M to $75M (effective 2026)
- Employer-provided childcare credit increased from $150,000 to $500,000 ($600,000 for eligible small businesses)

### Sources Verified

| Source | Date Accessed | Status |
|--------|--------------|--------|
| IRS Notice 2025-67 (IR-2025-111) | March 17, 2026 | Verified |
| IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-32 | March 17, 2026 | Verified |
| IRS OBBBA tax release (Rev. 2025-32 amendments) | March 28, 2026 | Verified |
| SSA COLA Fact Sheet 2026 | March 17, 2026 | Verified |
| CMS Fact Sheet Nov 2025 | March 17, 2026 | Verified |
| IRS Rev. Proc. 2025-19 | March 17, 2026 | Verified |
| Tax Foundation OBBBA analysis | March 28, 2026 | Cross-referenced |
| Harter Secrest & Emery OBBBA analysis | March 28, 2026 | Cross-referenced |
| IRS OBBBA Senior Deduction guidance | March 28, 2026 | Cross-referenced |

sections.title: How to Use This Manual
paragraphs: This manual is a standalone, comprehensive resource for mastering the complete Executive MBA curriculum while simultaneously applying every concept to financial services practice. No external platform, software, or supplementary material is required.
VISUAL   Three Modes of Use

KEY INSIGHT  Active Learning Strategy Research shows active engagement produces 3x better retention than passive reading. For each section: (1) Read the concept. (2) Cover the definition and try to state it yourself. (3) Work through the example before reading the solution. (4) Read the Financial Services Application and identify how it applies to your current role. (5) Complete the Mastery Check at the end of each chapter.
Chapter Map
ACCOUNTING Debits & Credits  |  Revenues & Expenses  |  Stocks & Bonds  |  Financial Statements  |  Ratios  |  Managerial Accounting  |  Excel

sections.title: Accounting
paragraphs: Accounting is the language of business. Every decision — from hiring an employee to acquiring a company — ultimately shows up in the financial statements. This chapter takes you from the fundamental accounting equation through financial statement analysis, ratio interpretation, and managerial decision-making tools like CVP analysis and activity-based costing.
VISUAL   The Accounting Cycle
VISUAL   Income Statement Waterfall
VISUAL   Balance Sheet Structure
Debits & Credits

SCENARIO  Opening Day at a New Business Sarah opens a consulting firm by investing $50,000 of personal savings and taking a $30,000 bank loan. She buys $20,000 of equipment and keeps $60,000 in cash. Balance sheet: Assets = $60,000 cash + $20,000 equipment = $80,000. Liabilities = $30,000 loan. Equity = $50,000 investment. Check: $80,000 = $30,000 + $50,000. Every transaction she makes from here must keep this equation balanced.

FORMULA   Accounting Equation Assets = Liabilities + Equity Assets: owned items. Liabilities: debts owed. Equity: owner investment + retained earnings. Worked Example: Assets $500K, Liabilities $300K. Equity = $200K. Borrow $100K: Assets $600K = Liabilities $400K + Equity $200K. Common Mistake: Forgetting that EVERY transaction requires two entries to keep this equation balanced.
Revenues & Expenses

SCENARIO  Accrual vs. Cash: Why It Matters A law firm completes a $50,000 case in December but the client pays in January. Under accrual accounting: $50,000 revenue in December (when earned). Under cash accounting: $50,000 revenue in January (when received). The December income statement looks completely different under each method. Most businesses use accrual because it better matches revenues with the expenses incurred to generate them.

FORMULA   Net Income Net Income = Total Revenues - Total Expenses Revenue: income from operations. Expenses: all costs of generating that revenue. Worked Example: Revenue $1M, COGS $400K, SG&A $200K, Interest $50K, Tax $87.5K. NI = $262,500. Common Mistake: Confusing cash received with revenue earned. Accrual accounting recognizes revenue when EARNED.
Stocks & Bonds

SCENARIO  FIFO vs. LIFO in a Rising Market A retailer bought 100 widgets at $10, then 100 more at $15. They sold 100 widgets. Under FIFO (first-in, first-out): COGS = $1,000 (sold the $10 units), ending inventory = $1,500. Higher profit, higher taxes. Under LIFO (last-in, first-out): COGS = $1,500 (sold the $15 units), ending inventory = $1,000. Lower profit, lower taxes. Same economic reality, different reported results. This is why understanding inventory methods matters for analysis.

FORMULA   Breakeven Point Breakeven Units = Fixed Costs / (Price - Variable Cost per Unit) Fixed costs: don't change with volume. Price - VC = Contribution Margin per unit. Worked Example: Fixed costs $100K. Price $50. VC $30. CM = $20. Breakeven = 5,000 units. Common Mistake: Assuming fixed costs stay fixed at all volumes. Beyond the relevant range, a second facility may be needed.
Financial Statements

FORMULA   Return on Equity ROE = Net Income / Total Equity Measures return generated on shareholder investment. Worked Example: NI $500K, Equity $2M = 25% ROE. Compare to industry average ~15%: this company is generating strong returns. Common Mistake: Comparing ROE across industries without context. Capital-intensive industries naturally have lower ROE.
Ratios

FORMULA   Current Ratio Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities Above 1.0 generally healthy. Below 1.0 = potential liquidity risk. Worked Example: CA $800K, CL $500K = 1.6. Company has $1.60 for every $1.00 due within a year. Common Mistake: Assuming higher is always better. Ratio of 5.0 may mean too much idle cash not being invested.
Managerial Accounting

FORMULA   Debt-to-Equity D/E = Total Liabilities / Total Equity Higher = more leveraged. Industry norms vary widely. Worked Example: Liabilities $3M, Equity $2M = 1.5. Moderate leverage for manufacturing; high for services. Common Mistake: Ignoring industry context. Banks operate at D/E of 10:1+ because their business model requires it.
Excel
Excel
Excel
Excel
Excel

CASE STUDY  CVP Analysis for a Product Launch Decision A company considers launching a new product. Price: $120. Variable cost: $75. Additional fixed costs: $450,000/year. Contribution margin = $45/unit. Breakeven = 10,000 units. Market research estimates demand of 15,000 units. Projected profit: 5,000 x $45 = $225,000. But if demand is only 8,000? Loss: 2,000 x $45 = -$90,000. The CVP analysis shows management needs confidence that demand exceeds 10,000 before greenlighting. Sensitivity: what price increase would be needed to break even at 8,000 units? ($450,000/8,000) + $75 = $131.25 — an 9.4% price increase.
Connections to Other Disciplines
KEY INSIGHT  Finance Financial statements are the inputs to valuation models. Every DCF analysis starts with the income statement and cash flow statement you learned to build here.
KEY INSIGHT  Strategy Ratio analysis is used for competitive benchmarking. Comparing your ratios to competitors reveals strategic strengths and weaknesses.
KEY INSIGHT  Marketing & Pricing CVP analysis from managerial accounting directly informs pricing decisions. Your breakeven point determines the minimum viable price.

[DATA AS OF: March 2026. Verify current rates before client-facing use.]

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Insurance Carrier Financial Analysis Statutory Accounting Principles (SAP) used by state regulators are more conservative than GAAP — assets valued lower, liabilities recognized earlier. When evaluating a carrier like National Life Group (AM Best A+, $57.4B AUM), read the statutory annual statement for surplus position, Risk-Based Capital (RBC) ratio (above 300% = strong), loss ratios, and investment portfolio composition. Understanding carrier financials lets you confidently recommend products backed by strong balance sheets.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Commission and Override Accounting First-year commissions on IUL products typically run 80-115% of target premium. Renewal commissions of 2-5% in years 2-10. As a manager, override income on team production represents 10-25% of their first-year commissions. Revenue recognition: commission income is earned when the policy is issued and first premium paid — not when the application is submitted. This distinction affects cash flow planning significantly.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Tax Treatment of Insurance Products IRC Section 7702 defines life insurance for tax purposes. Benefits: tax-free death benefits (Section 101(a)), tax-deferred cash value growth, tax-free policy loans (if not a Modified Endowment Contract). The MEC test (7-pay test) limits how much premium can be paid in the first 7 years. 1035 exchanges allow tax-free transfers between qualifying policies. Understanding these rules is essential for designing client solutions and explaining policy benefits accurately.

sections.title: Markets & Economies
paragraphs: Economics provides the framework for understanding how markets work, why prices change, what drives economic growth, and how government policy shapes the business environment. This chapter covers microeconomics (individual markets and decision-making) and macroeconomics (the economy as a whole, including growth, inflation, unemployment, and policy).
VISUAL   SWOT Analysis Framework
VISUAL   Supply & Demand Equilibrium
Supply & Demand

SCENARIO  How a Supply Shock Cascades Through Markets A drought destroys 30% of Brazil's coffee crop. Supply curve shifts left (less available at every price). At the old price, shortage emerges. Price rises to new equilibrium — higher price, lower quantity. Coffee shops face higher input costs. Some raise prices (passing cost to consumers). Others absorb it (lower margins). Some switch to tea offerings (substitution). This is exactly what happened in 2021 when frost damage caused a 50% price spike. Understanding supply/demand shifts lets you anticipate price movements in any market.

FORMULA   Price Elasticity of Demand Ed = (% Change in Quantity Demanded) / (% Change in Price) |Ed| > 1 = elastic. |Ed| < 1 = inelastic. |Ed| = 1 = unit elastic. Worked Example: Gym raises price 20%. Members drop 15%. Ed = -0.75 (inelastic). Revenue increases with price increase because quantity drops proportionally less. Common Mistake: Forgetting the revenue implication: elastic goods — price increase DECREASES revenue. Inelastic goods — price increase INCREASES revenue.
Economic Worldview

SCENARIO  Why Insurance Is Inelastic Price elasticity for term life insurance is approximately -0.3 (inelastic). A 10% premium increase causes only a 3% drop in policies sold. Why? Because insurance is a necessity for people with dependents, and switching costs are high (new underwriting, potential health changes). This means carriers can raise premiums with relatively small customer losses. For advisors, it means clients are more sensitive to perceived VALUE than to price — focus conversations on coverage adequacy, not premium shopping.

FORMULA   GDP GDP = C + I + G + (X - M) C: consumer spending. I: business investment. G: government spending. X: exports. M: imports. Worked Example: US GDP ~$28 trillion. Consumer spending is ~68% ($19T). Government ~17%. Investment ~18%. Net exports slightly negative (trade deficit). Common Mistake: Confusing GDP growth with wellbeing. GDP measures economic output, not distribution, sustainability, or quality of life.

CASE STUDY  Adverse Selection in Health Insurance The individual health insurance market exhibits classic adverse selection: people likely to need healthcare are more likely to buy coverage. Healthy people opt out, making the insured pool sicker, driving premiums higher, causing more healthy people to exit — a death spiral. The ACA addressed this with coverage mandates (forcing healthy people in) and premium subsidies. In life insurance, adverse selection is managed through underwriting: medical exams, health questionnaires, and risk classification ensure pricing reflects actual risk. Understanding market failures isn't just theory — it's the foundation of insurance product design.

sections.title: Finance
paragraphs: Finance answers the fundamental question: how do we make decisions about money across time? From evaluating whether a project is worth investing in, to determining how a company should fund itself, to valuing an entire business — finance provides the quantitative tools for value-based decision-making. The time value of money is the foundation: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow.
VISUAL   Capital Budgeting Decision Process
VISUAL   Capital Structure Spectrum
Time Value of Money

SCENARIO  The Power of Compounding Invest $10,000 at 8% annually. After 10 years: $21,589. After 20 years: $46,610. After 30 years: $100,627. Your original $10,000 grew 10x — but $90,627 of that growth came from returns on returns (compound interest), not your original investment. At 12%, the 30-year value jumps to $299,599. The 4% difference in return rate produces a 3x difference in outcome over 30 years. This is why even small differences in investment returns matter enormously — and why financial planning starts as early as possible.

FORMULA   Future Value FV = PV x (1 + r)^n PV: present value. r: rate per period. n: number of periods. Worked Example: $10,000 at 8% for 10 years: FV = $10,000 x 1.08^10 = $21,589. Common Mistake: Not matching rate to compounding period. Annual 8% with monthly compounding: use r=0.08/12, n=120.
Financial Management

SCENARIO  NPV vs. IRR: When They Disagree Project A costs $50K, returns $80K in year 1. IRR = 60%, NPV at 10% = $22.7K. Project B costs $500K, returns $700K in year 1. IRR = 40%, NPV at 10% = $136.4K. IRR says choose A (60% > 40%). NPV says choose B ($136.4K > $22.7K). Who is right? NPV. Project B creates 6x more value despite a lower percentage return. IRR measures efficiency; NPV measures value creation. When they conflict (different project sizes, different timing), ALWAYS follow NPV.

FORMULA   Present Value PV = FV / (1 + r)^n FV: future amount. r: discount rate. n: periods. Worked Example: $50,000 in 5 years at 10%: PV = $50,000/1.10^5 = $31,046. Worth paying up to $31,046 today. Common Mistake: Using wrong discount rate. Rate must reflect risk: government bond = risk-free; startup = much higher.
Capital Structure

FORMULA   Net Present Value NPV = Sum[CF_t / (1+r)^t] - Initial Investment CF_t: cash flow at time t. r: discount rate. Positive NPV = accept. Worked Example: Invest $100K. Returns $35K/yr for 4 years at 10%. NPV = $31.8K + $28.9K + $26.3K + $23.9K - $100K = $10.9K. Accept. Common Mistake: When NPV and IRR disagree (mutually exclusive projects), ALWAYS follow NPV.
Modigliani-Miller

FORMULA   WACC WACC = (E/V x Ke) + (D/V x Kd x (1-T)) E: equity. D: debt. V: E+D. Ke: cost of equity. Kd: cost of debt. T: tax rate. Worked Example: E=$60M, D=$40M, Ke=12%, Kd=6%, T=30%. WACC = (0.6x12%) + (0.4x6%x0.7) = 7.2% + 1.68% = 8.88%. Common Mistake: Forgetting to use AFTER-TAX cost of debt. Interest is tax-deductible, so 6% debt at 30% tax = 4.2% true cost.
Capital Budgeting

FORMULA   CAPM (Cost of Equity) Ke = Rf + Beta x (Rm - Rf) Rf: risk-free rate. Beta: stock volatility vs. market. Rm-Rf: market risk premium. Worked Example: Rf=3%, Beta=1.2, Market premium=7%. Ke = 3% + 1.2x7% = 11.4%. Common Mistake: Using historical beta for a company whose risk profile has changed significantly.
Value Creation

CASE STUDY  Regional Expansion Capital Budgeting A financial services firm evaluates two growth strategies. Option A: Hire 5 advisors at $30K each ($150K total), expecting $400K first-year production generating $80K override income, growing 15% annually. Option B: Invest $150K in digital marketing, expecting $200K leads generating $60K revenue year 1, growing 25% annually. At 12% discount rate, 5-year NPV of A = $127K, NPV of B = $148K. Despite A having higher first-year returns, B creates more total value due to higher growth rate. This demonstrates why NPV — not gut feel or first-year returns — should drive capital allocation.
Connections to Other Disciplines
KEY INSIGHT  Accounting Financial statements from Accounting are the inputs to every valuation. DCF starts with the cash flow statement; multiples analysis uses income statement data.
KEY INSIGHT  Strategy Capital budgeting is how strategy gets funded. Porter's frameworks identify WHICH opportunities to pursue; NPV determines WHETHER they create value.
KEY INSIGHT  Data & Decisions Probability distributions from Data & Decisions power scenario analysis in capital budgeting. Monte Carlo simulation uses probability to model investment uncertainty.
Financial Services Applications
[DATA AS OF: March 2026. Verify current rates before client-facing use.]

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Premium Financing NPV Analysis Client needs $5M death benefit. Annual premium $100K for 10 years. Loan rate: SOFR (3.65%) + 250bps = 6.15%. Policy crediting rate: 7.5% (1.35% spread). Year 1: loan balance $106,150, cash value ~$85,000. Year 5: loan ~$585K, cash value ~$475K. Year 10: loan ~$1.06M, cash value ~$1.15M (crossover). At death: $5M benefit minus loan payoff = net benefit. Stress test: at 5.5% crediting, crossover extends to year 15+. This is why running multiple scenarios is non-negotiable in premium financing.

FINANCIAL SERVICES  Agent Recruiting as Capital Budgeting Cost per recruit: $15K-30K (licensing, training, marketing support, time). Expected outcome: 35% probability agent reaches $100K production in 18 months. At 20% override: $20K annual return on $25K investment = 80% ROI if successful. Expected value: 35% x $20K = $7K expected return on $25K = 28% expected ROI. Still attractive but very different from headline 80%. This is portfolio thinking: recruit enough agents to let the law of large numbers work in your favor.
